acceptance: New file cli/internal/agent/script.go implements RunScript(opts) that reads a directive file, executes directives against opts.WorkDir using real filesystem and git operations, and returns a real Result. The harness is registered in cli/internal/agent/registry.go as `script` and marked always-available in Discover(). Directive grammar supports at minimum: append-line, create-file, delete-file, commit, set-exit, no-op, sleep-ms, fail-during. Environment variable interpolation supports ${DDX_BEAD_ID}, ${DDX_ATTEMPT_ID}, ${DDX_WORKER_ID}. Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go cover: (1) append-line + commit produces a real commit with the expected content, (2) no-op produces zero commits and exit 0, (3) create-file + commit produces the file and a commit, (4) set-exit 1 makes the run return exit 1, (5) fail-during 2 executes directives 0 and 1 but errors on directive 2, (6) env var interpolation replaces ${DDX_BEAD_ID} with the value from Correlation, (7) invoking via `ddx agent run --harness=script --model=<file>` works end-to-end with a real temp git repo. go test ./internal/agent -run TestScript completes in under 5 seconds.
description:
The execute-loop / execute-bead / land-coordinator system cannot be tested end-to-end today because every "fake" agent in the codebase returns a canned Result{} without actually writing files or making commits. `cli/internal/agent/virtual.go` is a prompt-response dictionary replayer — it returns canned stdout based on prompt hash but does not touch the filesystem or git. `fakeAgentRunner` and `fakeExecuteBeadGit` in `cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go` are in-memory mocks that track expected call counts. Neither produces actual commits that the orchestration layer can rebase, merge, or land.

The consequence: every test that claims to cover concurrency, serialization, or git orchestration uses mocked git and therefore cannot catch merge-race, rebase-conflict, or coordinator-serialization bugs. `TestConcurrentWorkersNoMergeRace` at execute_bead_orchestrator_test.go:228 is particularly misleading — it uses separate orchTestGitOps instances per worker so the shared-writer invariant is never exercised. The test passes because no shared state exists to race on.

## What this bead builds

A new deterministic `script` harness (cli/internal/agent/script.go, mirroring cli/internal/agent/virtual.go):

- Registered alongside claude/codex/agent/virtual in cli/internal/agent/registry.go
- Always available (no binary probe required)
- Takes a directive file (passed via RunOptions.Model or a new RunOptions.ScriptFile field — either works)
- Reads the directive file, executes each line against opts.WorkDir as real filesystem + git operations
- Returns a real Result{ExitCode, Output} that the loop and orchestrator can observe

## Directive grammar

Line-based, comments start with `#`. Each directive performs a real side effect:

```
append-line <path> <text>          # append text to file (create if missing)
create-file <path> <content>       # create or overwrite file
delete-file <path>                 # rm -f path
modify-line <path> <regex> <repl>  # sed-style in-place substitution
run <shell-command>                # exec in WorkDir with clean env
commit <message>                   # git add -A && git commit -m <message>
set-exit <code>                    # final exit code (default 0)
no-op                              # no commits, no files, exit 0
fail-during <index>                # synthetic failure at the Nth directive
sleep-ms <n>                       # deterministic delay (for scheduling tests)
```

Directives run in order. Environment variables DDX_BEAD_ID, DDX_ATTEMPT_ID, DDX_WORKER_ID are interpolated into text arguments so directives can be parameterized. Example:

```
append-line tests/smoke.txt "hello from ${DDX_BEAD_ID}"
commit "smoke: ${DDX_BEAD_ID}"
```

## Invocation

From the CLI: `ddx agent run --harness=script --model=./testdata/append-smoke.txt --prompt=/dev/null`

Programmatically from a test:
```go
runner := agent.NewRunner(agent.Config{Harness: "script"})
result, err := runner.Run(agent.RunOptions{
    WorkDir: wtPath,
    Model:   "testdata/append-smoke.txt",
    Correlation: map[string]string{"bead_id": beadID, "attempt_id": attemptID},
})
```

## Implementation notes

- Mirror the dispatch pattern in cli/internal/agent/runner.go:81 where `virtual` is intercepted
- Always-available in Discover() same as virtual + agent
- No external dependencies beyond what the agent package already uses (os, os/exec for git)
- Estimated size: ~200 lines of implementation + ~200 lines of tests
- Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go must prove each directive actually performs its claimed side effect on a real temp git repo

## What this enables

Every test case in beads B (integration tests) and C (server runWorker regression test) depends on this harness existing. Without it, the land-coordinator redesign ships behind whatever real claude happens to do on the day the test runs.
title: Implement deterministic script harness for orchestration stress testing

acceptance: dxx-agent has dedicated ls, find, and grep tools; stall detector classifies them as read-only; bash is only classified as write-capable; agents on OpenRouter models transition from reading to writing faster
description:
During execute-loop runs with OpenRouter models (GLM-5.1, Qwen 3.6), agents get stuck in read loops — they call read and bash (for ls/find/grep) repeatedly without ever writing. The stall detector cannot distinguish bash ls from bash "git commit" because bash is classified as a write-capable tool, resetting the stall counter on every call.

Adding dedicated read-only tools (ls, find, grep) to ddx-agent would:
1. Let the stall detector accurately distinguish read-only exploration from write activity
2. Reduce token usage by avoiding bash prompt/response overhead for simple file queries
3. Give models clearer affordances — read-only tools for exploration, write/edit/bash for modification
4. Help models transition from reading to writing faster by making the tool boundary explicit

With these tools, the stall detector classifies read/ls/find/grep as read-only (increment stall counter) and write/edit/bash as write-capable (reset counter). Bash is only used when the agent needs commands that might modify the filesystem.
notes: Stall detection exists. Gap: no dedicated ls/find/grep tools registered in agent_runner.go tools slice (only Read/Write/Edit/Bash). Add as distinct agentlib.Tool implementations.
title: Add dedicated read-only tools (ls, find, grep) to ddx-agent for stall detection and efficiency

acceptance: ddx agent log --bead axon-abc123 shows all execution attempts for that bead in chronological order with ATTEMPT, STARTED, DURATION, HARNESS, MODEL, OUTCOME, TOKENS, COST, SESSION columns. OUTCOME is resolved from result.json when available, falls back to exit_code. Summary line printed after table. --json emits filtered SessionEntry array with outcome field. Bead with no sessions prints 'no sessions found for bead <id>'. Existing ddx agent log behavior (no --bead flag) is unchanged. ddx agent log <session-id> single-session detail view is unchanged.
description:
ddx agent log is fully implemented (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go lines 745-807) and reads .ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl. Each SessionEntry has a Correlation map (cli/internal/agent/types.go) that includes bead_id when the session was created by execute-bead. However there is no way to filter sessions by bead — you must know the session ID in advance or scroll through all recent sessions.

This bead adds --bead <id> filtering and a per-bead attempt summary view to the existing command.

## Required changes

### 1. --bead flag on ddx agent log (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go)
When --bead <bead-id> is provided:
  - Scan all SessionEntry records in sessions.jsonl
  - Filter to entries where Correlation["bead_id"] == bead-id
  - Sort ascending by Timestamp (attempt order)
  - Display in attempt-history table format (see below)

No change to the default behavior (no --bead = show recent N sessions as today).

### 2. Attempt history table columns
ATTEMPT | STARTED | DURATION | HARNESS | MODEL | OUTCOME | TOKENS | COST | SESSION
- ATTEMPT: 1-based index in chronological order
- OUTCOME: merged / preserved / no-changes / error (from ExecuteBeadResult.Outcome via Correlation or result.json)
- SESSION: truncated session ID (8 chars) for use with ddx agent log <session-id>
- COST: formatted as /bin/zsh.0042 or 'local' if cost_usd == 0

### 3. Outcome resolution
SessionEntry does not directly store outcome — it stores exit_code and error. The full outcome is in .ddx/executions/{attemptID}/result.json (ExecuteBeadResult struct). When rendering the attempt table:
  - Use Correlation["attempt_id"] to find .ddx/executions/{attempt_id}/result.json
  - Read ExecuteBeadResult.Outcome from that file
  - Fall back to exit_code == 0 ? 'success' : 'error' if result.json is absent

### 4. Summary line
After the table, print a one-line summary:
  '<bead-id>: N attempts, M merged, K preserved, J errors. Last attempt: <elapsed> ago.'

### 5. --json passthrough
When --bead and --json are both set, emit the filtered SessionEntry array as JSON with an added outcome field resolved from result.json.

## Key files
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — newAgentLogCommand() lines 745-807, add --bead flag and filter path
- cli/internal/agent/types.go — SessionEntry struct (lines 94-122), Correlation map field
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go — ExecuteBeadResult struct (lines 30-64), artifact paths convention
- .ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl — runtime data source

description:
<context-digest>
Observed on 2026-04-11 while launching server-managed execute-loop workers against the restarted 8094 server. Worker records remained state=running with current_bead=null even after distinct execution bundles and manifests had already been created for claimed beads.
</context-digest>

Worker API projection lags behind real execution start. When a server-managed execute-loop worker has already claimed a bead and created an execution bundle, /api/agent/workers and /api/agent/workers/:id should expose that current bead immediately instead of waiting for end-of-run summary parsing.

## Scope
- Project current_bead as soon as the worker claims or launches a bead
- Keep worker detail/list views aligned with execution bundle state while a run is active
- Add regression coverage for running workers whose current bead is known before process exit

## Acceptance Criteria
Server-managed execute-loop workers expose current_bead promptly while running, not only after exit or summary parsing, and tests cover the live-running projection path.
title: Project current bead immediately for running server-managed workers

acceptance: `ddx agent execute-bead` resolves governing references for both doc-ID and repo-relative path-style `spec-id` values when the target file exists in the execution snapshot; the generated prompt/manifest include the resolved path reference instead of the `No governing references` fallback; and deterministic tests cover a bead whose `spec-id` is a repo-relative path such as `workflows/README.md`.
description:
<context-digest>
Alpha validation run from /Users/erik/Projects/helix using `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json` against a live HELIX bead whose `spec-id` is the repo-relative path `workflows/README.md`. Evidence includes `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/prompt.md`, `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/manifest.json`, and `cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go`.
</context-digest>

`ddx agent execute-bead` prompt synthesis failed to resolve a live path-style `spec-id` into governing references, so the generated execution prompt told the agent there were no governing references even though the referenced file exists in the execution snapshot.

## Reproduction
1. In `/Users/erik/Projects/helix`, inspect bead `helix-fef22846`; it carries `spec-id=workflows/README.md`.
2. Run `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json` or `ddx agent execute-bead helix-fef22846 --json`.
3. Open `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/prompt.md`.
4. Observe the prompt includes `- spec-id: workflows/README.md` but the `## Governing References` section says `No governing references were resolved from the current execution snapshot.`

## Observed gap
`executeBeadResolveGoverningRefs()` builds the doc graph and looks up only by graph document ID. Path-like `spec-id` values that point to real files in the repo are dropped instead of being resolved as governing references.

## Why this matters
This is exactly the context the execution prompt tells the agent to read before changing code or docs. When DDx drops an existing repo-relative path reference, agent execution becomes less grounded and the artifact bundle misstates the available governing context.
title: Resolve path-style spec-id values in execute-bead governing references

acceptance: Backend Go test in cli/internal/server/server_test.go: (1) registers the same project path 5 times with t.Setenv-isolated state dir, asserts GET /api/projects returns exactly 1 project with last_seen = the most recent registration. (2) registers a path inside a linked worktree (via git worktree add fixture), asserts the stored path is the PRIMARY worktree, not the linked path. (3) registers a project, deletes the path on disk, runs the reachability sweep (expose a Server.SweepProjects helper for tests), asserts the project is marked unreachable with a tombstone timestamp, then asserts GET /api/projects with default params hides it and ?include_unreachable=true shows it. Migration test: writes a state file with 50 duplicate entries for the same canonical path, starts the server, asserts the post-startup state file has exactly 1 entry. Acceptance smoke: running the migration on the eitri server's current state file reduces the count from 4186 to single-digit, verifiable by curl https://127.0.0.1:7743/api/projects | jq length.
description:
GET /api/projects currently returns 4186 entries on the eitri node. The real number of projects on this machine is single-digit. Root cause: the per-CLI-invocation auto-registration in serverreg.TryRegisterAsync registers the project on EVERY ddx command run with no dedupe, AND historical bugs (CWD drift in linked worktrees, the stale beads-store-write bug fixed in 26c0266) caused thousands of phantom 'projects' to land with paths like /Users/erik/Projects/ddx/.claude/worktrees/agent-XYZ. The registry has no GC, no path canonicalization, no reachability check.

Fix scope:
1. Path canonicalization on register: filepath.Abs + EvalSymlinks before storing, so /tmp and /Users/erik/Projects/.... are normalized
2. Detect linked-worktree paths (via git rev-parse --git-common-dir) and store the PRIMARY worktree path, never the linked worktree path itself — this is the same fix we did for FindNearestDDxWorkspace in commit 26c0266 but applied to project registration
3. Dedupe on register: if a project with the same canonical path already exists, update its last_seen instead of creating a new entry
4. Reachability sweep: a periodic background goroutine (or a manual ddx server projects gc command) that stat()'s each registered project's path and either (a) marks it as 'unreachable' with a tombstone timestamp or (b) removes entries whose path has been gone for >24h
5. Migration: on server startup, run dedupe + path canonicalization + reachability sweep ONCE against the existing state file so the eitri 4186 entries collapse to the real count
6. GET /api/projects gains a ?include_unreachable= query (default false) so the UI can hide tombstones unless asked
title: Server: dedupe and garbage-collect the project registry; tombstone unreachable projects

acceptance: catalog update populates costs for gpt-5.4 and other models; routing uses cost as tiebreaker when benchmarks unavailable; newer model versions preferred within same family
description:
OpenRouter exposes pricing for all models including subscription-tier ones (gpt-5.4, etc). We should:
1. Fetch ALL model prices from OpenRouter during catalog update, cross-reference by ID
2. For models not directly matchable by ID, store OpenRouter reference price separately as a capability signal
3. Use cost as directional capability indicator in routing when benchmark scores are unavailable: higher cost = likely higher capability
4. Add version ordering: within same model family, prefer newer version (opus-4.6 > opus-4.5) all else equal
5. Add ModelVersion field to ModelEntryYAML to encode family + version for comparison
6. Subscription/local harnesses still take priority (sunk cost), but when routing to pay-per-token (openrouter), use actual catalog prices for cost estimation
title: Use OpenRouter pricing as capability proxy and populate costs for all models

acceptance:
- [ ] Option A, B, or C selected and documented in an ADR or design note under the ddx repo
- [ ] Implementation lands with migration path for existing .ddx/beads.jsonl files (no lost bead history)
- [ ] Concurrent writes from 2+ workers on the same machine produce a deterministic, semantically correct tracker state
- [ ] Concurrent writes from 2+ machines (via ddx server or shared remote) produce a deterministic, semantically correct tracker state
- [ ] Claim races are resolved by exactly one winner; losers see a structured 'already_claimed' error
- [ ] Crash recovery: a claimed bead whose worker died returns to unclaimed after the heartbeat timeout (existing mechanism continues to work)
- [ ] Existing beads.jsonl format continues to work as a read-only snapshot for offline tooling (jq queries, etc.)
- [ ] Unit + integration tests covering the concurrency scenarios above
- [ ] Migration test: apply the upgrade to axon's current beads.jsonl (748+ beads on sindri side, 30+ new on eitri side) and verify no semantic loss
description:
Fourth child of ddx-b7ee0d6f. .ddx/beads.jsonl is a git-tracked mutable ledger. Multiple writers on different machines or in parallel worker processes produce divergent files that require manual textual merge. git merge can sometimes append-merge JSONL cleanly if each side touches disjoint lines, but when both sides touch the same bead (claim / close / add-event), the merge is semantically broken even when git reports it as resolved.

## Evidence

On axon, eitri and sindri both wrote to beads.jsonl independently for 14 days. Local has 134 commits touching the tracker; origin has 336. When I tried to rebase my planning commit onto origin, git reported conflicts in .ddx/beads.jsonl — and these conflicts would have been 'resolved' by git's textual merge logic even when the semantic outcome is wrong (e.g. two machines claiming the same bead with different claimed-machine values).

The existing execute-loop help text says:

    By default execute-loop submits to the running ddx server as a background
    worker and returns immediately. Use --local to run inline in the current
    process.

So a ddx server EXISTS and is supposed to coordinate. But .ddx/beads.jsonl is still the ledger, which means the server is not the source of truth — it's just a submission surface. The git-tracked file is authoritative, and that's what's racing.

## Options (pick one during implementation)

**A. Server-authoritative tracker.** When the ddx server is running, it owns beads.jsonl. All reads and writes go through the server's API. The git-tracked file becomes a periodic snapshot for backup/offline use, not the source of truth. Workers on multiple machines talk to a shared server.

**B. CRDT append-only log.** Replace the current representation with an append-only log keyed by (machine_id, bead_id, sequence, timestamp). Each writer appends to its own partition; readers compute the effective state by merging partitions with a conflict-resolution rule (last-writer-wins per (bead_id, field), or better). git textual merge of the partitioned log is always safe because writers never share lines.

**C. Per-bead lock files with atomic rename.** Each bead mutation acquires a lock file (beads/<id>.lock), writes beads/<id>.json atomically via rename, releases the lock. The JSONL aggregate is regenerated from per-bead files. Git tracks per-bead files, not the aggregate. Safe across local parallelism but not across machines without network coordination.

**Recommendation**: A is the cleanest if the ddx server is already deployed as authoritative infrastructure. B is the best fit for the current decentralized model. C is a stopgap that solves intra-machine parallelism but not multi-machine drift.

## Scope note

Do NOT conflate this bead with the fetch/push/rebase triad (ddx-bd42f640, ddx-83ec1430, ddx-6aa50e57). Those three fix the git-level divergence; this bead fixes the tracker-level divergence. Both are needed — the triad without tracker safety would still produce semantically-conflicting beads.jsonl merges even when the underlying git history is linear.

## Test cases

- Two workers on the same machine claim different beads concurrently: both claims persist correctly
- Two workers on different machines claim different beads concurrently: both claims persist correctly (requires network coordination)
- Two workers attempt to claim the same bead: one wins, the other sees 'already claimed' and picks a different one
- Recovery from crash between claim and CloseWithEvidence: the bead returns to unclaimed after a heartbeat timeout
- Tracker state is deterministic from any valid ordering of concurrent writes (CRDT option)
title: execute-loop: .ddx/beads.jsonl concurrent-write safety

acceptance: ddx agent execute-loop --harness claude --model claude-sonnet-4-6 invokes the claude binary, not ddx-agent; session log shows model: claude-sonnet-4-6 not model: vidar or model: bragi; if --model specifies an unrecognized claude model, the error is surfaced before any bead is claimed; ddx agent execute-loop --harness agent --model vidar continues to work as before (regression guard)
description:
When execute-loop is invoked with both --harness claude and --model <provider-name> (e.g. --model claude-sonnet-4-6), the --harness flag is silently ignored and the embedded agent harness is used instead, routing to whichever LM Studio provider is configured as default.

Observed: started execute-loop --local --harness claude --model claude-sonnet-4-6. Every session in the output showed 'model: vidar' or 'model: bragi' and all LLM responses were from qwen3.5-27b. No claude traffic was generated. The claude binary is healthy (ddx agent doctor reports OK). When execute-loop is started with --harness claude and NO --model flag, the claude binary is correctly invoked (confirmed by claude --print process appearing in ps output).

Root cause hypothesis: the --model flag value is being interpreted as a provider/route key by the embedded agent harness dispatch layer. When the model string matches a known provider name or a catalog entry that maps to the embedded-openai surface, the harness selection silently switches to 'agent' regardless of what --harness specifies. The --harness flag loses the race.

Required fix: --harness must take strict precedence over any routing implied by --model. If --harness claude is specified and the model string is not a valid claude model ID, the loop should either (a) pass the model string through to the claude binary as-is and let it fail with a clear error, or (b) emit a startup warning 'model X is not a recognized claude model; ignoring --model flag' and proceed with the harness default. Silent rerouting to a different harness is never acceptable.

Additionally: when the harness override causes a routing mismatch, the error or warning must appear before any bead is claimed, not after execution has started.

acceptance: Two execute-loop --local workers started simultaneously against the same project never execute the same bead; the second worker to attempt a claim on an already-claimed bead skips it and picks the next open one; a bead left in_progress by a crashed worker becomes reclaimable after a configurable TTL (default: 10 minutes)
description:
When two execute-loop workers scan the ready queue simultaneously, both can claim the same bead before either has committed its in_progress status, resulting in duplicated work or merge conflicts.

Observed: started two execute-loop --local --harness agent workers (vidar and bragi) against the same project. Both immediately began working on the same bead in separate worktrees. Their output files were in near-perfect lockstep (131 vs 127 lines), indicating they had independently claimed and started executing the same item. One was killed manually to stop the waste.

Root cause hypothesis: the claim operation in execute-loop reads the ready queue, selects the next open bead, then writes its status to in_progress as two separate steps. If two workers interleave between the read and the write, both see the bead as open and both proceed to execute it.

Required fix: bead claiming must be an atomic compare-and-swap. The worker should attempt to transition bead status from open → in_progress using a conditional write that fails if the status is no longer open at write time (optimistic locking). If the claim fails, the worker should re-scan for the next available bead rather than proceeding.

For local workers (beads.jsonl on disk), a file-level lock around the read-claim-write cycle is sufficient. For server-submitted workers, the DDx server should serialize claims.

Additional issue: when a worker is killed mid-execution, the bead may be left in_progress indefinitely. The loop should support a claim timeout or heartbeat so abandoned in_progress beads become reclaimable after a TTL.
title: execute-loop: bead claiming is not atomic — concurrent workers race on the same bead

acceptance: FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 define an agent dashboard showing configured providers, availability/health, utilization metrics, freshness, and unknown-state semantics; the design includes a queryable read model and deterministic fixture scenarios for Playwright coverage
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: server agent observability and operator controls. Evidence covers FEAT-002 server surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI, FEAT-014 token-awareness/routing metrics, the embedded-agent progress lane, and the requirement that a host+user ddx-server expose actionable provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.
</context-digest>
Define the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.

## Goals
- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known
- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps
- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window
- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed
- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces
- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty

## Required implementation planning
- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states
- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one page

description:
Same situational-awareness gap as the workers view: when N projects are in flight, the operator currently can only see one project's bead queue at a time via /nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/beads. Add a node-level combined beads view that merges all projects' queues into one filterable table.

Backing data: needs a new endpoint OR client-side merge. The cleanest design is server-side aggregation since clients shouldn't fan out N requests:
- New endpoint GET /api/beads (node-wide, no project scope)
- Returns: array of beads with an extra 'project_id' field on each entry
- Supports the same query params as /api/projects/:project/beads (?status=, ?ready=, ?label=, ?limit=)
- Walks the project registry from FEAT-020 and concatenates each project's bead store

UI requirements:
- Route: /nodes/:nodeId/beads (combined; the existing /nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/beads stays)
- Adds 'Project' column showing project short name
- Filter chips for status (open/in_progress/closed) and project (multi-select)
- Filter state encoded in URL query params so views are bookmarkable
- Auto-refresh every ~10s (slower than workers since beads change less frequently)

Delete-aware: when a project is removed from the registry its beads disappear from the combined view on the next refresh.
title: Dashboard UI: cross-project beads view (combined ready/in-progress/closed across all projects)

acceptance: execute-bead prompt contract explicitly invites no_changes with a rationale. The fake-agent test harness seeds a no_changes rationale file; execute_bead_test.go asserts ExecuteBeadResult.NoChangesRationale is populated; execute-loop uses the rationale to classify 'already_satisfied' when it references a prior commit SHA or test name; the rendered log (ddx server workers log) shows the rationale on its own line; 'agent made no commits' no longer appears without context.
description:
Today when execute-bead exits with no_changes, the operator sees 'agent made no commits' and nothing else. That's useless for triage — the operator cannot tell whether:
- the bead is genuinely already satisfied (work present in a prior commit)
- the agent gave up because it couldn't find the relevant files
- the agent classified the task as ambiguous or too risky
- the agent hit a tool limit or context cap
- the prompt contract was unclear

The fix is to make no_changes a structured result that carries a short rationale from the agent itself, written at the point where the agent decided not to commit. Example of what the operator should see:

  status:    no_changes
  rationale: The epic filter requested by this bead was already added in
             commit 1da6495 (cli/internal/bead/store.go readyFiltered). I
             reviewed the current code and tests, confirmed the filter
             excludes 'epic' beads from ReadyExecution, and verified
             TestReadyExecutionExcludesEpics exists and passes. Nothing
             to do. Ref: 1da6495, TestReadyExecutionExcludesEpics.

This is specifically the case that motivated this bead: a continuous-drain worker processed ddx-b44ca394 ('Prevent execute-loop from launching open epics by default') after the epic filter had already been implemented on main. The agent instead made a synthesized no-op commit that only retargeted 17 .claude/skills/helix-* symlinks, which the loop classified as 'success' because a commit was produced. Had no_changes carried a rationale pointing at the prior commit, the loop could have closed the bead as 'already_satisfied' and moved on cleanly.

Scope:
1. Execute-bead prompt contract must explicitly instruct the agent: if you determine the bead's work is already present or cannot be done, report that in a structured way — do NOT make a synthesized commit to 'land something'. Today the prompt says 'Making no commits (no_changes) should be rare' which actively pushes agents toward the wrong behavior.
2. The agent's exit mechanism needs a way to carry a rationale string back. Simplest version: the agent writes a file to the worktree like .ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/no_changes_rationale.txt before exiting. ExecuteBead reads it if no commits were produced and attaches the text to the result.NoChangesRationale field.
3. The ExecuteBeadResult struct gains a NoChangesRationale string field, populated only when outcome == no_changes. Status: success and the unused 'reason' field need not change.
4. execute-loop close semantics: when no_changes arrives with a non-empty rationale pointing at a prior commit, the loop can optionally close the bead as already_satisfied with the rationale recorded in the close event body (instead of waiting for MaxNoChangesBeforeClose=3 strikes). Gate this behind a 'rationale references existing commit' heuristic to avoid flapping — if the rationale is a vague 'nothing to do', keep current cooldown behavior.
5. ddx server worker log should render the rationale prominently, not bury it in session metadata.
6. The prompt contract note that no_changes should be 'rare' is replaced with: 'If the work is already present, say so and provide a reference (commit SHA, test name, file path). A well-justified no_changes is always preferred to a cosmetic synthesized commit.'
title: no_changes reports must include a rationale the operator can act on

acceptance:
DDx docs or Scans the current project's execution-ready bead queue, claims the next
ready bead, runs ddx agent execute-bead from the project root, records the
structured result, and continues until no unattempted ready work remains.

Usage:
  ddx agent execute-loop [flags]

Flags:
      --effort string            Effort level
      --from string              Base git revision to start from (default: HEAD)

  -h, --help                     help for execute-loop
      --json                     Output loop result as JSON
      --model string             Model override

Global Flags:
      --config string              config file (default is $HOME/.ddx.yml)
      --library-base-path string   override path for DDx library location
  -v, --verbose                    verbose output explain how execute-loop chooses the next bead when multiple execution-ready beads exist, including any priority/stable-order tie-breakers; if current behavior is not deterministic enough for workflow consumers, the command or docs are updated so the ordering contract is explicit and testable

Review area: execute-bead merge eligibility semantics. Evidence covers FEAT-006 step 8-11, FEAT-007 execution-document discovery, FEAT-010 required execution behavior, TD-005 ratchet semantics, and API-001's separation between structural readiness checks and post-run result classification.
</context-digest>

## Goals

acceptance:
- [ ] New command or subcommand: `ddx doctor divergence` or `ddx divergence check` prints the divergence report
- [ ] Thresholds are configurable via .ddx/config.yaml (default: 5 commits each side, 7 days merge-base age)
- [ ] execute-loop refuses to start in ERROR state unless --force passed
- [ ] Structured event 'divergence_status' emitted at loop start and on every doctor run
- [ ] Optional webhook integration via config.yaml alert_webhook_url
- [ ] Unit tests: each state (clean, ahead, behind, diverged, network-down, drift)
- [ ] Integration test: run the loop on a diverged repo and observe the refuse-to-start behavior
- [ ] Docs: README section on divergence monitoring, thresholds, and how to recover
description:
Fifth child of ddx-b7ee0d6f. The 14-day silent divergence on axon (eitri local 134 commits ahead of merge-base, sindri/origin 336 commits ahead) was caught only because a human happened to try pushing. A trivial monitor running a few times a day would have flagged the problem at 5 commits, not 470.

## Required behavior

Add a lightweight divergence check that runs either:
- At the top of each execute-loop iteration (cheap, ~50ms), OR
- Periodically via a scheduled task (ddx doctor or similar), OR
- Both

The check computes:
- `git fetch origin <branch>` (tolerant of network failure)
- local_ahead = `git rev-list --count origin/<branch>..HEAD`
- origin_ahead = `git rev-list --count HEAD..origin/<branch>`
- merge_base_age = `git log -1 --format=%ar $(git merge-base HEAD origin/<branch>)`

Emit a structured event 'divergence_status' with these fields. If:
- local_ahead > threshold (default 5): warn
- origin_ahead > threshold (default 5): warn
- both > 0: ERROR — diverged, neither side is a strict ancestor
- merge_base_age > threshold (default 7 days): warn (drift)

Integration surface:
- `ddx doctor` prints the divergence report
- `ddx agent execute-loop` refuses to start if in the ERROR state (both sides ahead) unless --force is passed
- An optional webhook / notification channel (config: alert_webhook_url) for ops teams

## Why this is P1 not P0

The fetch/push/rebase triad (P0) prevents NEW divergence. This bead catches EXISTING divergence and alerts before it grows. Without the P0 fixes, this alert would fire constantly; with them, it should fire rarely. Still valuable as defense in depth.

## Test cases

- Fresh clone: report is clean (0 ahead / 0 behind)
- Local 3 commits ahead: report shows local_ahead=3, no warning
- Local 6 commits ahead: report shows local_ahead=6, warning emitted
- Both sides ahead: ERROR status, execute-loop refuses to start
- Merge base 10 days old with both sides at 0: drift warning, execute-loop continues
- Network unreachable: report shows 'unknown', no ERROR, execute-loop continues
title: execute-loop: divergence monitoring and alerting

acceptance: ddx doctor (1) reports the absolute path of the currently-executing binary via os.Executable(); (2) checks whether that path matches $HOME/.local/bin/ddx (the install.sh canonical location); (3) walks PATH and reports every other 'ddx' found, flagging any whose SHA-256 differs from the running binary as a warning; (4) suggests 'rm <stale> && cp <current> <stale>' or 're-run install.sh' to fix. Test covers the multi-binary-on-PATH scenario by shadowing a fake ddx in a temp dir earlier on PATH.
description: install.sh installs the ddx binary to $HOME/.local/bin/ddx as the canonical location. But users can end up with multiple copies (e.g. /home/erik/bin/ddx from a stale install, /usr/local/bin/ddx, or a dev build on PATH ahead of the canonical one). This session had a real incident: /home/erik/bin/ddx was 053171bf (older) while /home/erik/.local/bin/ddx was 40f8db0d (current) — the shell picked the wrong one for hours, causing confusion about whether blocker fixes were actually active. ddx doctor should detect mismatches between the running binary's path and the canonical install location AND warn when multiple ddx copies exist on PATH with different SHAs.
title: ddx doctor should verify running binary is in canonical install location

acceptance:
library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md and .ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md are rewritten to match the HELIX testing concern and the DDx concerns.md override. All of the following must be true:

(1) Mechanical grep guards:
- `grep -n "Mock all external dependencies" library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md` returns empty
- `grep -n "test doubles (mocks" library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md` returns empty
- `grep -n "Use real implementations where possible" library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md` returns empty (the integration section must use "zero mocks, no exceptions" not "where possible")
- `grep -ciE "stub|in-memory|extracted interface|contract test" library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md` returns at least 10
- `grep -c "99%" library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md` returns at least 1 (the "mocks are a worst practice, avoid in 99% of cases" phrasing is present)
- `grep -c "script harness" library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md` returns at least 1
- `grep -c "docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md" library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md` returns at least 1 (cites DDx concerns)
- `grep -c "workflows/concerns/testing" library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md` returns at least 1 (cites HELIX concern)
- `grep -c "orchTestGitOps\|fakeExecuteBeadGit\|gateTestGitOps" library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md` returns at least 1 (drift signals section names the DDx-specific anti-patterns by type)

(2) Structural requirements:
- Unit Tests section explicitly states that mocks are a worst practice and names the "extract interface, write in-memory stub, test with contract suite" pattern as the default.
- Integration Tests section states "zero mocks, period, no exceptions" and cites the script harness as the ONLY approved fake.
- Contract Tests section contains a code block showing the "same suite against stub + real backend" pattern (Go-idiomatic, idiomatic for the DDx codebase).
- New Drift Signals subsection lists at least 10 anti-patterns including testify/mock imports, gomock for first-party interfaces, per-goroutine mock git, and the named offenders in DDx's test suite.
- The word "mock" appears only in prohibitions and in the Drift Signals subsection. The body of the Unit/Integration/Contract sections uses "stub," "in-memory implementation," or "real backend" exclusively.

(3) Source/installed sync:
- library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md and .ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md are byte-identical after the fix.

(4) Regression witness:
- A new test fixture or smoke check somewhere in the repo (e.g., a lefthook pre-commit or a doc-validation script) greps library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md for the forbidden phrases above and fails the commit if any reappear. This prevents silent drift if someone later reverts.

(5) Changelog entry or commit message:
- The commit message explicitly references the HELIX testing concern and the DDx concerns.md override, explains why mocks are being removed from the persona, and cites at least one concrete DDx anti-pattern (orchTestGitOps / fakeExecuteBeadGit / TestConcurrentWorkersNoMergeRace) that the old persona produced.
description:
The test-engineer-tdd persona at library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md promotes mocks as standard practice, directly contradicting the HELIX testing concern that DDx declared active in concerns.md on 2026-04-05. Every agent that adopts this persona produces mock-heavy tests that violate the concern's rules. This is how DDx accumulated `orchTestGitOps`, `fakeExecuteBeadGit`, `gateTestGitOps`, `TestConcurrentWorkersNoMergeRace`, `mockExecutor`, and every other test-that-lies-about-its-coverage pattern the opus test plan review surfaced.

## Provenance

- Persona authored 2025-09-15 in commit a4cc8ac as part of FEAT-011 persona system build
- Testing concern adopted in DDx 2026-04-05 in commit b711ac8 (`Add testing concern`)
- The persona has been silently contradicting the concern for 9 days with no audit
- Personas are a DDx-native concept (HELIX has no personas/ directory in workflows/); this is DDx's problem to fix

## The core principle

**Mocks are a worst practice and should be avoided in 99% of cases.** The right pattern, always, is: extract an interface at your domain boundary, write an in-memory stub implementation of that interface, and write a contract test suite that runs against BOTH the stub and the real backend. When both pass the same suite they are proven interchangeable. This exposes hidden assumptions, keeps stubs honest, and produces tests that assert behavior instead of call sequences.

Mocks — defined specifically as call-recording test doubles that assert method X was called Y times with args Z — test implementation instead of behavior. They break when internals change without user-visible consequences. They give false confidence: a passing mock test proves nothing about the system's actual behavior because the thing under test never interacts with a real dependency. Most notoriously in DDx: mock git tests have proven zero merge-race, rebase-conflict, or serialization invariants despite claiming to.

The 1% of cases where mocks are acceptable: third-party SDK boundaries where a stub is infeasible (payment processors with real-world-only callbacks, email delivery, SMS, external auth providers). Even there, prefer contract tests against a sandbox environment when one exists.

## Offending sections

library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md lines 90-94 (Unit Tests):

```
### Unit Tests
- Test individual functions/methods in isolation
- Mock all external dependencies
- Focus on business logic
- Use test doubles (mocks, stubs, spies)
```

`Mock all external dependencies` is absolute and wrong. The HELIX testing concern says:

> Prefer stubs (canned return values) over mocks (call-sequence assertions). Stubs test behavior; mocks test implementation.
> Use real implementations where practical: real databases via testcontainers or Docker Compose, real HTTP via local servers, real file systems via temp directories.
> Mock only at true system boundaries: third-party APIs, payment processors, email services, external auth providers.
> Never mock the thing you are testing.

`Use test doubles (mocks, stubs, spies)` lists mocks alongside stubs as equivalent. They are not equivalent. Mocks assert call sequences (implementation detail). Stubs return canned values (behavior contract). The persona should never put them on the same line.

library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md lines 96-100 (Integration Tests):

```
### Integration Tests
- Test component interactions
- Use real implementations where possible
- Test database operations
- Verify API contracts
```

`Use real implementations where possible` is weakly worded. DDx's project override in concerns.md states:

> Integration tests: zero mocks. No exceptions. Integration tests exercise real production implementations of every dependency in the graph — real git (t.TempDir() + scrubbed GIT_* env), real filesystem, real bead store, real coordinator, real server, real agent harness (the script harness is the only approved fake for the AI-provider boundary, and it does not mock — it performs real filesystem and git operations deterministically). A test that substitutes any mock for a production component is not an integration test. It is a unit test that is lying about its coverage.

The persona must echo this in absolute language, not "where possible."

## What to change

### Rewrite the Unit Tests section

Replace lines 90-94 with guidance that matches the HELIX concern and DDx override exactly. Required content:

- The code under test depends on interfaces at domain boundaries. Extract the interface if it does not exist.
- Isolate the code under test with in-memory STUB implementations of those interfaces. Stubs return canned values the test sets up. They never record call sequences.
- Every stub must be backed by a contract test suite that the real implementation ALSO passes. Same suite, both implementations. That proves they are interchangeable.
- Never use testify/mock, gomock, mockery-generated interfaces for assertion purposes, or any call-sequence assertion library. Assert on behavior — what did the code do to observable state? — not on call counts.
- Never mock git, filesystem, bead store, database, queue, or any first-party infrastructure — use real implementations in temp directories.
- The only approved "fake" in DDx unit tests is an in-memory implementation of a first-party interface you own.
- Mocks are a worst practice. Avoid them in 99% of cases in favor of extracted interfaces with in-memory stubs. The 1% exception is third-party SDK boundaries where a stub is infeasible.

### Rewrite the Integration Tests section

Replace lines 96-100 with:

- Integration tests use zero mocks. Period. No exceptions.
- Real database in a temp dir, real filesystem via t.TempDir(), real git via git init in a temp dir with scrubbed GIT_* env, real HTTP via a local test server, real queues, real bead store, real coordinator, real server.
- The only approved fake in the DDx suite is the `script` harness (cli/internal/agent/script.go) as the AI-provider boundary replacement. It is not a mock — it performs real filesystem and git operations deterministically against the worktree.
- A test that substitutes any mock for a production component is not an integration test. It is a unit test that is lying about its coverage.
- Never mock the thing you are testing. A coordinator test that substitutes a fake LandingGitOps is mocking the thing under test; use RealLandingGitOps against a real git repo.

### Add a Contract Tests section

The persona currently has a Contract Tests section at lines 102-106. Rewrite it to cover the "stub must pass the real implementation's contract suite" pattern, not just "APIs meet specifications." Example structure:

```go
// contract_test.go — runs the same suite against both implementations
func TestBeadStoreContract(t *testing.T) {
    cases := []struct {
        name  string
        build func(t *testing.T) Store
    }{
        {"memory", func(t *testing.T) Store { return NewMemoryStore() }},
        {"file",   func(t *testing.T) Store { return NewFileStore(t.TempDir()) }},
    }
    for _, c := range cases {
        t.Run(c.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            s := c.build(t)
            t.Run("CreateAndGet", func(t *testing.T) { /* ... */ })
            t.Run("UpdateLosesNoFields", func(t *testing.T) { /* ... */ })
            t.Run("ConcurrentWritersSerialized", func(t *testing.T) { /* ... */ })
        })
    }
}
```

If the stub passes and the real backend fails, the backend is broken or the stub is lying. Either way the contract suite exposes the drift immediately. This is how DDx keeps stubs honest.

### Add a Drift Signals subsection

Mirror the HELIX testing concern's drift signals and add DDx-specific anti-patterns. Any agent reviewing code that contains these should reject or refactor:

- `testify/mock` imports in new test files
- `gomock` generated mocks for first-party interfaces
- Structs named `*Mock*`, `fake*Git`, `fake*Store`, `orchTest*GitOps`, `gateTest*GitOps` that implement a first-party interface for assertion purposes (in-memory stubs for first-party interfaces are OK and encouraged, as long as they are real implementations, not call recorders)
- Per-goroutine mock git in concurrency tests (the shared-writer invariant is not being tested)
- test.skip, .skip(), @Ignore, @Disabled without a linked issue
- Mock-heavy tests that assert call order rather than observable state
- Static JSON fixture files (use factory functions with fakers instead)
- Tests named `test1`, `testHelper`, `it works` (rename to describe behavior)
- `expect(true).toBe(true)` or tautological assertions
- Commented-out tests (git has the history; delete them)
- Retry loop around a flaky test (fix the flake)

### Add citations to authoritative sources

The persona must explicitly cite:

- **HELIX testing concern**: `workflows/concerns/testing/concern.md` in HELIX (or `.ddx/plugins/helix/workflows/concerns/testing/concern.md` when installed) — the upstream authority for the stubs-over-mocks rule.
- **DDx concerns.md**: `docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md` — the DDx-specific application of the rule, including the zero-mocks-in-integration-tests override.
- **Script harness**: `cli/internal/agent/script.go` — the canonical deterministic fake agent.
- **Integration test reference**: `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_integration_test.go` — the reference pattern that every new integration test should follow.

### Update the installed copy

library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md is the source of truth. The installed copy at .ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md must also be updated so downstream projects (HELIX and others) that depend on the DDx plugin pick up the fix. Either (a) update both files in the same commit and let the install process pick up the new source the next time it runs, or (b) run the library sync mechanism as part of the bead work.

## Why this matters

Every test in DDx's history that was written by an agent following this persona produced mock-heavy code. Those tests passed CI, appeared to cover invariants, and silently proved nothing. The alpha14 → alpha16 session has been the first time anyone audited them end-to-end and discovered the drift. Without this fix, the next agent adopting the persona will reproduce the same broken pattern.

The cost of fixing the persona is hours. The cost of NOT fixing it is weeks of debugging "why doesn't the land coordinator work" when the real answer is "because every test that 'proved' the old merge path worked was a lie."
title: test-engineer-tdd persona contradicts testing concern (promotes mocks)

The ddx-agent harness emits per-tool-call progress lines (→ llm request, ← llm response, 🔧 tool) that the worker log surfaces in real time. The claude harness should do the same.

Options:
1. Switch claude harness to --output-format stream-json and parse events as they arrive, writing progress to a sidecar file in the execution dir

acceptance: The DDx docs define `ddx agent execute-bead` as a clean-worktree workspace transform from `W` to `W'`; the bead is specified as the executable transform contract rather than a vague tracker ticket; DDx docs no longer assign governing-artifact resolution or prompt construction to DDx at runtime; a generic bead execution-readiness validation surface is specified with clear DDx-owned vs HELIX-owned checks; the docs define the success/failure state machine including structural validation, required post-run checks, preserve-on-failure evidence, and rebase+fast-forward landing on success; and the contract explicitly remains valid for future multi-project `ddx server` worker pools rather than assuming a single served repo
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: execute-bead semantics, executable bead contracts, readiness validation, and DDx/HELIX boundary ownership. Evidence covers FEAT-006, FEAT-010, FEAT-013, SD-012, server-driven execution discussions, and cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go.
</context-digest>
Define DDx bead execution around the functional model `W' = execute(bead, W)`, where `W` is a clean git-backed workspace input state and `ddx agent execute-bead` materializes that state in an isolated worktree before mutation.

## Required design outcomes
1. Clarify that `ddx agent execute-bead` starts from a resolved base revision, treats the caller's dirty worktree as outside the transform, checkpoints it separately, and executes only against the isolated worktree state.
2. Define the bead as the executable transform contract: the bead must carry the instruction/prompt, acceptance, declared context inputs, execution policy, landing/preservation policy, and post-run validation policy needed for replayable execution.
3. Unwind DDx designs that currently imply DDx derives governing artifacts, prompt construction, or workflow policy from the live document graph at execution time. DDx should provide composable graph/exec tools, not hidden workflow orchestration.
4. Define a generic DDx execution-readiness validation surface for beads, such as `ddx bead validate --execution` or equivalent inspection semantics, that checks structural executability without embedding HELIX-specific policy.
5. Specify the success/failure state machine around execution: structural validation, agent execution, post-run validity checks / required executions, preserve-on-failure evidence semantics, and land-on-success semantics.
6. Make the automation boundary explicit: future humans, HELIX flows, or `ddx server` worker pools must consume the explicit bead contract and validator output rather than relying on hidden prompt assembly or runtime artifact inference.
7. Preserve room for multi-project server execution: the contract must not assume a single served repo or a single server process, and must remain valid when project-scoped worktrees are managed by a multi-project `ddx server`.
8. Make HELIX's responsibility explicit: HELIX should author the executable bead contract and create a new contract document in `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/` that defines the DDx/HELIX boundary for executable bead execution. The contracts directory does not currently exist in this repo, so this work must establish it.

## Spec and design scope
- Reconcile FEAT-006 agent-service workflow with the workspace-transform model
- Reconcile FEAT-010 execution-definition language so execution selection and required checks are explicit input or separately composed query output, not hidden bead-context derivation
- Preserve FEAT-013's substrate-not-orchestration boundary while still supporting async server-managed worker pools
- Align SD-012 git-awareness wording with the actual clean-input/worktree model, checkpoint semantics, and preserve-vs-land outcomes
- Establish how the generic execution-readiness surface is consumed by future automation without embedding HELIX policy in DDx

## Expected artifact work
- New contract under `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/` for the executable bead boundary
- Updates to the affected feature / solution design docs so they all describe the same execution contract
- Follow-on design beads for server-managed supervision and multi-project server topology
- Follow-on implementation beads only after the contract is settled
title: Define execute-bead workspace-transform contract and readiness validation

acceptance: Concurrent execute-bead workers no longer checkpoint unrelated DDx runtime churn from other active attempts into their base revisions; the snapshot boundary for DDx-owned files is documented and enforced; and concurrency tests cover the reproduced multi-worker case.
description:
<context-digest>
Observed while running multiple execute-loop workers concurrently on 2026-04-11. Replacing git stash with checkpoint commits fixed the immediate beads.lock/workers race, but execute-bead still starts from a dirty repo root where .ddx/beads.jsonl and other DDx-owned execution artifacts may be changing because of other live workers. That means one attempt can inherit unrelated DDx runtime/evidence churn into its checkpoint base.
</context-digest>
Tighten execute-bead checkpointing so an attempt's input snapshot is not polluted by unrelated DDx-generated state from other live executions.

## Scope
- Define which DDx-owned paths are part of the execution input snapshot vs excluded runtime churn
- Prevent unrelated live tracker/evidence/worker mutations from being captured into a new attempt base by default
- Add concurrency-focused tests covering simultaneous workers and dirty DDx-owned state
- Keep the tracked execution-evidence contract coherent with the new snapshot boundary
title: Keep execute-bead checkpoint bases free of unrelated DDx runtime churn

description:
ddx agent execute-loop fails on eitri: agent harness routes to z-ai/glm-5.1 ignoring local vidar config

ddx agent execute-loop consistently fails on the helix machine (eitri) with provider-level errors.
The execute-loop correctly selects and claims beads, but the agent harness never runs the work.
CRITICAL: ddx agent run --harness agent works perfectly on the same machine, pointing to a
model-resolution bug specifically in execute-bead/execute-loop.

PROOF: direct agent run works, execute-loop fails:
  ddx agent run --text "Hello" --harness agent --timeout 60s → succeeds with qwen3.5-27b on vidar
  ddx agent execute-loop --once --json --harness agent → fails with z-ai/glm-5.1 via OpenRouter 404

ROOT CAUSE: Model resolution in execute-bead injects --model z-ai/glm-5.1, overriding the
native ~/.config/agent/config.yaml provider config (default_provider: vidar, model: qwen3.5-27b).
The z-ai/glm-5.1 model is not defined in models.yaml or the native config — it appears to come
from stale execute-bead routing metadata. Even explicit --model flags on execute-loop don't
propagate correctly to the spawned execute-bead subprocess.

PROVIDER STATUS on eitri:
- vidar (http://vidar:1234/v1, qwen3.5-27b): WORKS — confirmed with ddx agent run
- bragi (http://bragi:1234/v1, qwen3.5-27b): DOWN — connection refused
- grendel (http://grendel:1234/v1, qwen3.5-27b): DOWN — connection refused
- openrouter (https://openrouter.ai/api/v1): FAILS — 404 guardrail restriction on claude-haiku

MACHINE CONTEXT (eitri, Linux):
- Hostname: eitri, paths use /home/erik/ and linuxbrew
- ~/.config/agent/config.yaml defines providers with default_provider: vidar
- models.yaml defines code-high profile → agent.openai: gpt-5.4 (OpenRouter surface)
- No ~/.ddx.yml config file
- No project-level .agent/config.yaml
- Codex and claude harnesses nearly out of quota — reserved for review, not bulk execution
- execute-loop was working previously (existing ddx beads show successful runs)
- z-ai/glm-5.1 string appears nowhere in source — comes from runtime routing metadata

ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES: No AGENT_PROVIDER, AGENT_BASE_URL, AGENT_API_KEY, or AGENT_MODEL set.
All routing comes from native ~/.config/agent/config.yaml.

IMPACT: ddx agent execute-loop is the primary execution surface for HELIX beads. If the only
working provider (vidar → qwen3.5-27b) cannot be used from execute-loop, entire queues drain
as failures. The loop records each with retry_after (6 hours) and retries indefinitely.

SUGGESTED FIXES:
1. Fix execute-bead model resolution: respect native .agent/config.yaml provider config or
   properly propagate the --model flag from execute-loop → execute-bead
2. Add provider health checks: ddx agent doctor should test the actual provider with a
   minimal API call, not just check binary existence
3. Fail open on provider errors: when the default provider fails, fall back to the next
   available provider in config rather than hard-failing
4. Guardrail error clarity: surface OpenRouter 404 as "provider unavailable due to
   account restrictions" rather than generic API error
5. Execute-loop resilience: when all providers fail, mark bead truly blocked instead of
   infinite retry_after cycles

FILES INVOLVED:
- cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go (resolveNativeAgentProvider, resolveAgentConfig, resolveModel)
- cli/cmd/agent_execute_loop.go (execute-loop model flag propagation)
- cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go (execute-bead model override logic)
- ~/.config/agent/config.yaml (machine config with vidar provider)
notes: Duplicate of ddx-c1822c89. Same root cause: execute-loop model routing ignores native harness config and picks catalog-tier model instead of locally-configured endpoints.
title: execute-loop provider failures on helix machine: OpenRouter guardrail 404 + Alibaba 502

description:
<context-digest>
Review area: server agent observability and operator controls. Evidence covers FEAT-002 server surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI, FEAT-014 token-awareness/routing metrics, the embedded-agent progress lane, and the requirement that a host+user ddx-server expose actionable provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.
</context-digest>
Define the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.

## Goals
- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known
- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps
- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window
- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed
- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces
- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty

## Required implementation planning
- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states
- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one page

acceptance: When `gitleaks` is unavailable, the `lefthook` fallback secret scan still catches obvious secret assignments but does not fail on ordinary `.ddx/beads.jsonl` tracker updates; the fallback inspects staged additions rather than whole files or otherwise avoids re-flagging historical tracker content; and a deterministic repro covers a staged tracker diff containing `token-awareness.md` text without any added secrets.
description:
<context-digest>
Tracker-only DDx bead commit from /Users/erik/Projects/ddx after filing execute-loop alpha findings. Environment does not have `gitleaks`, so the `lefthook` pre-commit hook used the fallback grep path in `lefthook.yml`.
</context-digest>

The fallback `secrets` hook in `lefthook.yml` produces false positives on staged `.ddx/beads.jsonl` changes because it scans the entire staged file with a broad `token|auth|password|...` regex instead of scanning added lines with tighter assignment-oriented patterns.

## Reproduction
1. Ensure `gitleaks` is not installed.
2. Stage a normal tracker-only change to `.ddx/beads.jsonl`.
3. Run `git commit`.
4. Observe the `secrets` hook fails and prints a line from `.ddx/beads.jsonl` where text like `token-awareness.md` and later quoted JSON content satisfy the fallback regex even though no secret was added.

## Observed gap
The fallback pattern is broad enough to match ordinary prose containing words like `token` or `auth`, and because it scans the whole staged file it re-trips on historical tracker content whenever `.ddx/beads.jsonl` changes.

## Why this matters
In environments without `gitleaks`, normal bead creation becomes effectively uncommittable. That blocks DDx's own tracker workflow and makes it harder to land the very bugs discovered during alpha execution.
title: Fix fallback secret scan false positives on tracker JSONL commits

acceptance: Backend Go test in cli/internal/server/workers_test.go starts a worker against a fake executor that completes 3 beads (2 success, 1 failure) with a 50ms delay between each. Polls Server.workers.Show(id) every 30ms and asserts that BEFORE the loop exits, at least one Show response reports Attempts >= 1. Final Show after exit reports Attempts=3, Successes=2, Failures=1. Smoke acceptance: with a running worker, ddx server workers show {id} reports a non-zero Attempts count within 30 seconds of the first bead completing, not after the entire loop exits.
description:
ddx server workers show <id> reports Attempts: 0  Success: 0  Failed: 0 for a worker that has been running for 30+ minutes and has visibly completed multiple beads (per the worker log stream and per landed commits in main). The counters only update at the end of worker.Run(), when the loop exits. While the loop is mid-flight the server WorkerRecord stays at zero, making the show endpoint useless for live progress.

Root cause: in cli/internal/server/workers.go startWorker(), the call site is:

    loopResult, err := worker.Run(ctx, ...)  // BLOCKING — only returns when loop exits
    // THEN updates record.Attempts = loopResult.Attempts

The ExecuteBeadLoopResult is built up in a local variable inside the loop Run() (see cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go line 147 'result := &ExecuteBeadLoopResult{}', incremented at line 255 'result.Attempts++' etc.) but the server holds no reference to it during execution.

Fix options:

A) ExecuteBeadLoopOptions gains a Progress field of type pointer to ExecuteBeadLoopResult. The caller pre-allocates one, passes it in, and the loop writes to it under a sync.Mutex. The server reads from the same pointer (with the mutex) when serving GET /api/agent/workers/{id}.

B) ExecuteBeadLoopOptions gains an OnBeadResult callback that fires after each bead. The server subscribes and updates record.Attempts/Successes/Failures incrementally.

C) The server already passes an EventSink and the loop already emits bead.result events to it. The server can subscribe to its own sink (parse the JSONL events) and update the counters from those events.

Option A is simplest and least invasive. Option C is cleanest because it reuses the existing event stream.
title: Server worker record Attempts/Successes/Failures stuck at 0 until loop exits

Review area: execute-loop no-progress control flow. Evidence covers the new `no_changes` result status, repeated one-shot queue runs reselecting the same bead, and the need for queue-drain behavior that either closes already-satisfied work or suppresses immediate retry on unresolved no-op attempts.

Implement no-change adjudication and retry suppression for execute-loop.

## Goals
- Run the defined satisfaction check after a `no_changes` execute-bead result
- Auto-close beads only when the satisfaction check says the bead is already satisfied
- Leave unresolved beads open but record retry suppression / cooldown so the next queue pass can move on
- Persist enough evidence and state for operator visibility and deterministic tests

acceptance: cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go ExecuteBead() contains ONLY: worktree setup (or accept pre-made worktree), prompt construction, agent harness dispatch, progress event emission, and a final completion event. No Merge, no UpdateRef, no preserveRef, no evaluateRequiredGates, no outcome classification beyond {task_succeeded, task_failed, task_no_changes}. All merge/preserve/gate logic moved to a new parent-side orchestrator that can be run interactively. Regression test: two workers on two different beads complete in arbitrary order; parent process merges both sequentially with interactive conflict resolution if needed; no worktree-internal merge code paths are exercised.
description:
The current execute-bead worker does WAY too much: creates a worktree, runs the agent, evaluates required gates, decides merge-by-default vs preserve vs land-conflict, writes preserve refs, synthesizes commits on IsDirty, classifies 7+ outcomes, manages heartbeats, handles claim/unclaim contention, runs recoverOrphans on stale worktrees. This surface is why (a) two concurrent workers race on the main-branch merge and the loser's good work ends up under refs/ddx/iterations/ needing manual rescue, (b) no_changes gets misclassified as synthesized-commit success, (c) the gate-enforcement path and the merge path are entangled, and (d) the worker needs special privileges (update-ref, merge, rebase) that a simple task-runner should never have.

The correct design is the one the operator just described: execute-bead is a thin worker. 'I have been handed a worktree and a task. I will execute that task and make commits in the worktree. I will stream progress events to ddx server. I will notify ddx server when I am done, reporting the worktree branch and the commit(s) I made. Then I exit.' That's it.

Everything else — merge to main, conflict resolution, required-gate evaluation, preserve-under-ref, decide whether to close the bead — moves to the PARENT process (the operator, or a driver tool running interactively in the terminal). Merge conflicts are resolved interactively the same as any other merge. A human (or an outer agent with full git authority) reviews the agent's commits and decides what to do with them.

This simplification deletes most of ExecuteBead() in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go and moves:
- resolveBase / baseRev tracking → parent
- Merge() call → parent
- preserveRef / UpdateRef / refs/ddx/iterations/ writes → parent
- evaluateRequiredGates → parent (or a separate 'ddx agent check' step)
- outcome classification 'merged/preserved/land-conflict/error' → parent
- recoverOrphans → parent (cleanup runs when parent next launches workers)
- heartbeatIsStale reclaim logic → parent (parent tracks worker health)

What the worker keeps:
- Take a worktree dir + task description (prompt) as input
- Launch the agent harness with the prompt
- Stream progress events to ddx server (session log + structured JSONL)
- On exit, emit a final event: worktree path, branch, commit shas (or empty), exit code, duration
- That's it

Execute-loop likewise shrinks or goes away. In the clean design, a driver loop in the parent process picks ready beads, creates a /tmp worktree per bead, calls 'ddx agent execute-bead' (worker-only mode), waits for the worker's exit event from ddx server, reviews the result, and merges or discards. When two beads are in flight concurrently, the parent serializes the merge step — no race possible.
title: Simplify execute-bead worker: task-only, no merge/preserve/gate logic

acceptance:
- [ ] cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go Merge function is replaced (or supplemented) with a Rebase function that takes (dir, base, head, onto) and rebases head onto onto starting from base
- [ ] On clean rebase, master fast-forwards to the rebased tip; one commit per bead
- [ ] On conflict, commits are preserved under refs/ddx/iterations/<bead>/<timestamp>/ via the existing iterationRef helper, the bead is unclaimed, the worker loop stops (same shape as the fetch-divergence path)
- [ ] The 'chore: checkpoint before merge' commit is removed entirely; a dirty worktree at landing time is a hard error with a structured message
- [ ] No code path in the execute-loop ever calls `git merge --no-edit` or any merge strategy — verified by grep
- [ ] LAND-LOCK REQUIREMENT: while a rebase + ff-push sequence is in progress for a given projectRoot, no other rebase + ff-push sequence may run against the same projectRoot. The serialization point can be (a) a per-project flock on .git/ddx-land.lock, (b) a single goroutine in the ddx server process that owns all rebase-land operations for that project (preferred — this matches the meta-bead ddx-8746d8a6 coordinator design), or (c) an equivalent mutex held across fetch → rebase → ff-push. Without this, two workers whose rebases complete simultaneously will both try to advance master to different SHAs and one will lose. The test for this requirement: spawn N (N>=5) concurrent land calls on the same projectRoot and assert that (i) exactly one commit is on master per submission after all calls return, (ii) the submissions are processed in some total order (not a set of parallel races), (iii) any submission that loses the race is either retried against the new tip OR preserved under refs/ddx/iterations/ with a clear reason — never silently dropped
- [ ] Unit test: two sibling workers, second rebases onto first's tip, master has exactly 2 new commits in linear order with no merge parents
- [ ] Unit test: conflicting rebase preserves commits and emits the land_conflict status
- [ ] Unit test: dirty worktree at landing time errors out without producing a checkpoint commit
- [ ] Integration test: running 5 parallel workers on the same machine for 10 iterations produces exactly 50 linear commits on master with no merge parents anywhere in the reflog

This bead is subsumed by the meta-bead ddx-8746d8a6 "execute-loop: adopt human-PR landing model end-to-end". The coordinator pattern in the meta-bead IS the implementation of this bead's rebase + land-lock requirements. If ddx-8746d8a6 lands first, close this bead as satisfied by the coordinator's rebase step. If this bead lands first, its implementation should be the foundation for the coordinator.
description:
Third child of ddx-b7ee0d6f. When two workers run in parallel on the same machine (each in its own execute-bead worktree), and both complete and attempt to land, the orchestrator uses `git merge --no-edit` with the ort strategy to fold one worker's commits into master. This produces a merge commit tree instead of a linear history, and triggers repeated 'chore: checkpoint before merge' commits when the working tree isn't clean.

## Evidence (source)

cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go:59-69 defines the Merge function:

    // Merge merges rev into dir's working tree via git merge. It commits any...
    func Merge(dir, rev string) error {
        _ = osexec.Command("git", "-C", dir, "commit", "-m", "chore: checkpoint before merge").Run()
        ...
        out, err := osexec.Command("git", "-C", dir, "merge", "--no-edit", rev).CombinedOutput()
        if err != nil {
            _ = osexec.Command("git", "-C", dir, "merge", "--abort").Run()
            return fmt.Errorf("merge: %s: %w", strings.TrimSpace(string(out)), err)
        }
    }

## Evidence (symptom on axon)

git reflog master on axon's eitri machine shows 12+ `Merge made by the 'ort' strategy` entries on 2026-04-13 within a 2-hour window, each one merging a sibling SHA that never appeared on origin:

    master@{8}  merge 5eb67fd0afab40ba4f9b06a8228760d69187af9d
    master@{12} merge 77999324c59bb715ee6ef290255a5b31e17b1930
    master@{13} merge 79772765312e3bc5a0715a6d9a2860016463e594
    master@{17} merge 268f63283af2df6bfde94200b5159222ea4f996d
    ... and nine more

Interleaved with 'chore: checkpoint before merge' commits. This is the signature of two+ parallel workers each producing sibling branches that get merged back into master one at a time, each merge preceded by a defensive checkpoint.

## Required behavior

Replace merge-based landing with rebase-based landing:

1. When a worker completes, its worktree is at commit W (child of base B)
2. Between B and now, master may have advanced to M (due to sibling workers or fetch-from-origin)
3. Instead of `git merge --no-edit W` on master, do:
   a. `git rebase --onto M B W` in the worker's worktree (or equivalent plumbing via `git cherry-pick B..W` onto M)
   b. On conflict: preserve the worker's commits under refs/ddx/iterations/<bead>/<timestamp>/ (existing infrastructure at execute_bead_orchestrator.go:21-26), unclaim the bead, stop the worker loop
   c. On clean rebase: fast-forward master to the rebased tip
4. NO checkpoint commits. If the master worktree is dirty, that's a bug in the preceding workflow (should have been committed by the worker) — fail loudly, don't paper over with a checkpoint

## Why rebase over merge

- Linear history — no fan of merge parents from parallel runs
- Conflicts surface earlier (during rebase, not as a semantically-broken merge)
- Each bead produces exactly one commit on master; a clean log of 'this bead → this commit'
- Push-ff-only from the sibling push bead (ddx-83ec1430) works because history stays linear

## Interaction with fetch bead

With ddx-bd42f640 ensuring HEAD is current with origin before work starts, and this bead ensuring intra-machine landings are linear, a worker's commit sequence is always: fetch → linear base → work → rebase → push. No merges, no sibling fans.

## Remove the checkpoint-before-merge pattern

The 'chore: checkpoint before merge' commits exist because the Merge function tries to handle a dirty worktree by committing it first. In the rebase model, a dirty worktree is illegal — the worker must have committed everything before the landing phase. If the worktree is dirty at landing time, error out immediately with a clear message about the worker not having committed cleanly. Do not silently checkpoint.
title: execute-loop: rebase sibling workers instead of git merge --no-edit (ort)
