acceptance: New file cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_integration_test.go contains at minimum the 9 test cases enumerated in the description. Shared helper newScriptHarnessRepo lives in cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go. Every test uses the script harness (not virtual, not fakeAgentRunner) and a real temp git repo created via git init with scrubbed GIT_* env. Every test verifies its assertion by running real git commands on the temp repo (git log, git rev-list, git for-each-ref) — no mock git. The suite runs under `go test ./internal/agent -run Integration -race -count=1` in under 60 seconds with all cases passing. Test 5 (FiveConcurrentBeads) proves linear history invariant that ddx-6aa50e57 acceptance demands. Test 6 (TwoWorkersSameBead) proves atomic claim invariant that ddx-3315dce2 acceptance demands. Test 7 (RebaseConflict) depends on the land coordinator from ddx-8746d8a6 being in place — it may be a t.Skip if the coordinator hasn't landed yet, with a TODO comment and a bead reference.
description:
Build the integration test suite for execute-loop orchestration using the script harness + real git temp repos. This is the test gap the opus test-plan review identified as the most critical coverage hole: every "concurrency" or "merge race" test today uses in-memory fakes with per-worker orchestrators, so the shared-writer invariant the meta-bead design depends on is never actually exercised.

## Test infrastructure helper

Build a shared helper `newScriptHarnessRepo(t *testing.T) (projectRoot, initialRev string)` in a new file `cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go` that:

1. Creates a temp dir via t.TempDir()
2. Runs `git init` with scrubbed GIT_* env (so the test's own pre-commit hook env doesn't leak)
3. Creates and commits an initial seed commit on `main`
4. Seeds .ddx/beads.jsonl with N ready beads (parameterized by test)
5. Returns projectRoot + the initial SHA

The helper must NOT inherit any GIT_DIR from the parent test environment.

## Test cases

Create cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_integration_test.go with these cases. Each runs against the script harness and real git:

1. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_SingleBead_AppendLine_Merged** — one bead, directive appends a line and commits, asserts git log main shows +1 commit, worktree is cleaned up, bead closed with closing_commit_sha.

2. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_NoOp_ClassifiedAsNoChanges** — directive no-op, asserts outcome=no-changes, bead stays open (below adjudication threshold), no commit on main.

3. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_DirtyWorktreeSynthesized** — directive creates files but omits explicit commit, asserts SynthesizeCommit fires, outcome=merged, single synthetic commit on main with the expected files.

4. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_FailedExit_WithCommits_Preserved** — directive commits then set-exit 1, asserts outcome=preserved, refs/ddx/iterations/<bead>/<ts>-<sha> exists, main unchanged.

5. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_FiveConcurrentBeads_LinearHistory** — seed 5 distinct beads, spawn 5 workers concurrently (single store, single projectRoot), assert `git log --merges main | wc -l` is 0, `git rev-list main ^initial | wc -l` is 5, every bead closed. **This is the ddx-6aa50e57 acceptance test that cannot be written today.**

6. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_TwoWorkersSameBead_ClaimedOnce** — seed 1 bead, spawn 2 workers against the same store, assert exactly one executes and the other reports no_ready_work. **This is the ddx-3315dce2 acceptance test.**

7. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_RebaseConflict_Preserved** — two beads both modify the same line in the same file, workers start from the same base, first lands cleanly, second gets rebase conflict and is preserved under refs/ddx/iterations/. Main has exactly +1 commit, ref exists for bead 2. (Only passes once the land coordinator from ddx-8746d8a6 is in place.)

8. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_ContextCancelBetweenIterations** — two beads, cancel context after first completes, assert second bead is not claimed. **This is the regression for commit 21d16e6.**

9. **TestIntegration_ScriptHarness_NoChangesRationale_ClosesBeadFast** — directive writes a no_changes_rationale.txt citing a commit SHA, asserts the bead closes as already_satisfied without the 3-strike cooldown wait.

Each test runs in isolation, cleans up its temp dir, and completes in under 10s. The full suite runs in under 60s. Use `-race` to catch any coordinator goroutine races.

## Out of scope

- Server-layer wiring (that's bead C)
- Real-AI acceptance tests (gated tier 4)
- Multi-project isolation (a separate bead, not filed here)
- Cross-machine scenarios (multi-machine topology bead)
title: Integration tests for execute-loop orchestration using script harness + real git

acceptance: A worker claiming a bead updates execute-loop-heartbeat-at every HeartbeatInterval while the bead is in_progress. A second worker scanning the queue reclaims a bead whose heartbeat is older than HeartbeatTTL and resets it to open before claiming. A bead with no heartbeat and claimed-at older than ClaimTTL is also reclaimable. Conditional write prevents TOCTOU race between two workers both detecting the same stale bead. A structured log entry is written when a stale bead is reclaimed. Existing in_progress beads written before heartbeat was introduced are reclaimable via the ClaimTTL fallback. Tests: (1) worker goroutine killed mid-execution leaves heartbeat timestamp in the past; second worker reclaims it; (2) two workers simultaneously detect a stale bead; only one successfully reclaims it; (3) active worker heartbeat prevents reclaim by another worker.
description:
When an execute-loop worker crashes mid-execution, the bead it was working on is left in status=in_progress indefinitely. The only recovery today is manual: ddx bead unclaim <id>. This blocks the queue from making progress on that bead.

Root cause: cli/internal/bead/store.go Claim() writes claimed-at (RFC3339) and claimed-pid once at claim time and never updates them. There is no heartbeat, no TTL check, and ReadyExecution() in execute_bead_loop.go skips in_progress beads unconditionally.

## Required changes

### 1. Heartbeat writes (cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go)
While a bead is in_progress, the owning worker goroutine must periodically update a heartbeat field in bead.Extra. Recommended field name: execute-loop-heartbeat-at (RFC3339). Write interval: every 30s (configurable via HeartbeatInterval on the loop config struct). The heartbeat goroutine runs alongside the agent executor and is cancelled when the bead completes or errors.

### 2. Stale claim detection (cli/internal/bead/store.go or ReadyExecution)
ReadyExecution() currently skips all in_progress beads. Change it to also yield beads where:
  - status == in_progress
  - execute-loop-heartbeat-at is set AND is older than HeartbeatTTL (default: 3 * HeartbeatInterval = 90s)
  - OR claimed-at is older than ClaimTTL (default: 10 minutes) AND execute-loop-heartbeat-at is absent (supports beads claimed before heartbeat was introduced)

When a stale bead is yielded, the claiming worker should first reset status to open via a conditional write (matching the stale heartbeat timestamp to guard against a TOCTOU race) before claiming it as its own. If the conditional write fails, skip and continue scanning.

### 3. Stale-claim log entry
When a worker reclaims a stale bead, write a structured log entry to the worker log: { event: 'stale-claim-reclaimed', bead_id, previous_claimed_machine, previous_claimed_pid, previous_heartbeat_at, stale_for_seconds }.

### 4. Config surface
Expose HeartbeatInterval and ClaimTTL on the loop config. For --local workers, read from .ddx/execute-loop.toml if present. CLI flags: --heartbeat-interval (default 30s) and --claim-ttl (default 10m).

## Key files
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go — main loop, claim call at line 124, add heartbeat goroutine
- cli/internal/bead/store.go — Claim() at line 439, ReadyExecution() stale check
- cli/internal/bead/types.go — StatusInProgress constant, Extra field conventions
title: Worker heartbeat and stalled-bead TTL reclaim for execute-loop

The polling alternative (GET /api/projects/:project/workers/:id with recent_phases) must also be served.

description:
<context-digest>
Review area: embedded-agent configuration ownership. Evidence covers ddx's partial `agent.agent_runner` mirror in `.ddx/config.yaml`, the native `ddx-agent` config model in `.agent/config.yaml`, and the need to support existing provider/backends such as OpenRouter without duplicating or losing configuration surface.

Replace DDx's lossy embedded-agent config mirror with native `ddx-agent` configuration loading.

## Goals
- Make `.agent/config.yaml` the authoritative config source for the embedded `agent` harness
- Define precedence between native agent config, environment variables, DDx orchestration config, and CLI overrides

Evidence covers cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go landing behavior, FEAT-006's required execution and ratchet language, FEAT-010 execute-bead compatibility requirements, and the need to consume authored execution documents rather than hidden workflow policy.

Implement the documented post-run merge-gate behavior in execute-bead.

## Goals
- Resolve applicable graph-authored execution documents from the governing snapshot used for the iteration
- Run the required execution documents after the agent completes and before land
- Preserve instead of land when a required execution fails or a ratchet gate blocks the iteration
- Record gate outcomes in the execute-bead result/evidence surface and through the existing exec history/runtime surfaces
- Add automated coverage for merge-by-default, required gate failure, and successful gate-passing land behavior

acceptance: Web UI route /nodes/:nodeId/agents (or /workers) lists all workers from /api/agent/workers with auto-refresh every ~2s. A worker row links to /nodes/:nodeId/agents/:workerId with three panels: prompt, log stream (structured events rendered with FormatSessionLogLines symbols), utilization (tokens/cost/duration/attempts). Both pages work against the alpha14 server without any additional backend work beyond optionally exposing the prompt content. Playwright test covers: list-shows-running-worker, list-refreshes-on-state-change, detail-renders-prompt-log-and-utilization.
description:
The server knows everything about running workers (state, harness, model, bead, attempts, prompt file, structured event log, token/cost/duration) via /api/agent/workers endpoints but the web UI has no live workers view. When an operator selects the eitri node they should land on (or reach in one click) an Agents page that lists every running worker from /api/agent/workers with bead-id, harness, model, state, elapsed, and latest status line. Clicking a row should open a worker detail page showing: (1) the rendered bead prompt from .ddx/executions/<attempt>/prompt.md, (2) the structured session log stream from .ddx/agent-logs/agent-loop-<session>.jsonl rendered via FormatSessionLogLines (so tool calls, turn counts, and timings are readable), (3) a session utilization panel with input/output/total tokens, cost-if-available, duration, attempts and successes/failures from the worker record, and (4) the base-rev and result-rev link once the worker has made progress. Polling at ~2s is fine for v1; no WebSocket required.

Backing endpoints that already exist:
- GET /api/agent/workers -> list
- GET /api/agent/workers/:id -> detail
- GET /api/agent/workers/:id/log -> stdout+stderr streams

Missing backing endpoints (if not already there): GET /api/agent/workers/:id/prompt to serve prompt.md content without requiring the UI to know the execution bundle path; optionally /api/agent/workers/:id/events to serve the structured JSONL stream if /log doesn't already cover it.
title: Dashboard UI: live workers page with per-worker prompt, log, and utilization detail

acceptance: A shipped single-project loop monitors one project for execution-ready beads, validates the selected candidate with the generic execution-readiness surface, executes it via ddx agent execute-bead, runs the required post-run checks, preserves failed or conflicted attempts without mutating the target branch, lands successful attempts by rebase plus fast-forward, resets to the updated branch tip, and continues scanning; automated coverage exercises at least success, preserved failure, land-conflict, and no-ready-work cases
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: single-project execute-bead automation. Evidence covers FEAT-006, FEAT-010, SD-012, API-001, the closed single-project loop design bead ddx-8bb9af7c, and the requirement to ship a tested single-project execution loop before multi-project or multi-host expansion.
</context-digest>
Implement the first shipped single-project loop that continuously works one project's executable-bead queue using the explicit execute-bead contract.

## Scope
- Monitor one project queue for execution-ready beads using the documented queue-ordering contract and the generic execution validator as a gate
- Run `ddx agent execute-bead` for the selected bead from the project root; do not introduce a second worktree lifecycle around the command
- Run required post-run validity checks and execution checks before classifying the attempt
- On execution failure, validation failure, or land conflict, preserve the iteration and evidence exactly as the execute-bead contract specifies, leave the target branch untouched, record the non-landed result, and continue scanning
- On success, land by rebase plus fast-forward, reset loop state to the updated branch tip, and continue scanning for the next executable bead
- Support a minimal control surface suitable for standalone use now and ddx-server hosting later without pulling in multi-project scheduling

## Boundaries
- Single-project only for the first release
- No inline PTY policy or hidden HELIX scheduling heuristics
- Multi-project worker pools and multi-host control planes remain follow-on work
title: implement: single-project execute-bead loop

acceptance:
- [ ] New function gitPushLanded(projectRoot, defaultBranch) called after successful CloseWithEvidence in execute_bead_loop.go
- [ ] Push uses --ff-only; never --force or --force-with-lease
- [ ] Push rejection triggers land_conflict + unclaim + refs/ddx/iterations/ preservation + stop-the-loop
- [ ] Network failure triggers 'push_pending' attempt marker but does NOT fail the bead; subsequent workers on the same branch see the pending state and halt
- [ ] Unit tests: successful push, rejected push, network failure
- [ ] Integration test: two workers racing, one wins the push, the other is rejected and unclaims its bead
- [ ] Integration test: repeated loops push each successful bead immediately (origin advances after every success)
- [ ] Docs updated: success path includes push; land_conflict path is the canonical 'two machines raced' outcome
description:
Second child of ddx-b7ee0d6f. When a worker successfully lands a bead (status = success), the new commit sits on the local branch forever. There is no git push. The source in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_commit.go has ZERO references to push/fetch/origin (grep verified). Result: successful work on one machine is invisible to every other machine, and the local branch grows indefinitely with unsynced commits.

## Evidence

cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_commit.go defines the commit step but never calls git push. The only mention of 'git' in that file is a comment about trailers.

cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go at the success branch (around line 343):

    if report.Status == ExecuteBeadStatusSuccess {
        if err := w.Store.CloseWithEvidence(candidate.ID, report.SessionID, report.ResultRev); err != nil {
            return result, err
        }
        result.Successes++
        result.LastSuccessAt = now().UTC()
    }

The tracker is updated (CloseWithEvidence writes to beads.jsonl) but the working tree / commits are never pushed to origin.

## Required behavior

After a successful landing (merge outcome = 'merged' AND bead status = success), the worker must:

1. Run `git push --ff-only origin <default-branch>`
2. On rejection:
   - Classify as ExecuteBeadStatusLandConflict (this status already exists in execute_bead_status.go)
   - Preserve the commits under refs/ddx/iterations/ (the infrastructure already exists for the preserved-result case)
   - Unclaim the bead so a later attempt can pick it up again AFTER divergence is resolved
   - Emit a 'push_rejected' event with the local HEAD and the remote tip
   - Stop the worker loop (divergence requires human attention)
3. On network failure: similar to fetch — log a warning, mark the attempt with a 'push_pending' flag, continue
4. Pair this push with the fetch-first bead (sibling ddx-bd42f640) so the loop is: fetch → verify ancestry → run bead → push — a closed consistency loop

## Why --ff-only

Force-with-lease is tempting but wrong here — if push is rejected, it means another machine pushed in the meantime, and we want to detect that and reconcile, not overwrite. --ff-only ensures we only ever advance origin, never rewrite it. A rejection is a signal, not a problem to work around.

## Test cases

- Fresh clone, worker lands a bead, push succeeds, origin advances to new HEAD
- Another machine pushed between fetch and commit (race): second worker lands, push is rejected, bead is unclaimed and marked land_conflict, loop stops
- Network unreachable: push is deferred, attempt records 'push_pending', worker continues but subsequent workers halt on the same branch
- Default branch is configurable
title: execute-loop: push to origin on successful bead landing

acceptance: A single per-project land coordinator goroutine owns all writes to target refs. Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/ (or cli/internal/server/) prove: (1) happy path — worker submits base=A result=B, coordinator fetches current tip X, rebases B onto X, fast-forwards, asserts the new tip is the rebased commit and no merge commits exist in the history between A and the new tip (use git log --merges --count to assert 0); (2) rebase conflict path — worker submits a commit that cannot rebase cleanly, coordinator preserves the submission under refs/ddx/iterations/<bead-id>/<attempt-id> and returns a "preserved" status, target branch is unchanged; (3) serialization path — 5 concurrent submissions against the same project against base=A produce 5 sequential rebased commits on the target branch with linear history (git log --merges returns 0), and the coordinator processes them in order; (4) server runWorker integration — a server-managed worker whose task produces a commit B actually advances the target branch to a rebased B, via the coordinator path, visible in git log main -1 after the worker exits; (5) --local mode integration — the --local execute-loop path runs a process-local coordinator that provides the same semantics. Secondary acceptance: RealOrchestratorGitOps.Merge is deleted from execute_bead_orchestrator.go OR clearly marked as the legacy path to remove; no production code path calls git merge --no-edit on the target branch; FEAT-006, API-001, FEAT-012 still describe the same contract (rebase + ff-only, linear history). Operator-visible smoke: running ddx agent execute-loop --harness claude --model sonnet against a seeded queue produces a linear history on main with no merge commits between the run's first and last commit.
description:
Today's execute-loop orchestration is a pile of patches on a flawed mental model. The user's target model is "human picks PR off the queue → rebases onto current tip → merges → next worker starts from the new tip." The code implements "worker branches off whatever main was when it started → runs task → git merge --no-edit that result back → 2-parent merge commit fan." The two models are incompatible; no patch to the second model gets you to the first.

Evidence the current model is wrong:

1. FEAT-006 §"Landing" (line 414), API-001 §"Landing & Preservation" (lines 217-222), and FEAT-012 §"Execute-Bead Git Operations" (line 413) all explicitly require rebase + fast-forward with linear history. The code in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go:61 Merge() runs `git merge --no-edit` with a defensive `chore: checkpoint before merge` commit — zero rebase, zero ff-only, zero fetch, zero push. Three spec files aligned on one model; code implements a different one.

2. cli/internal/server/workers.go:runWorker (around line 237) calls agent.ExecuteBead via an inline ExecuteBeadExecutorFunc closure at workers.go:248 and never calls LandBeadResult. LandBeadResult is only wired into two call sites: cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1372 (the `--local` execute-loop path) and cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go:134 (the interactive single-bead CLI). The default `ddx agent execute-loop` path (which submits to the server) produces worker commits that are NEVER landed on main. Commits sit in worktree refs and the loop reports "success" to the tracker anyway.

3. The "worker simplification" commit 49c3d2b that closed ddx-679d2fc7 moved Merge/UpdateRef out of execute_bead.go's GitOps interface into a new OrchestratorGitOps in execute_bead_orchestrator.go. That is a code-level function split, not a process-level split. The "parent orchestrator" runs synchronously in the worker process from whichever call site reaches it first. Two workers racing on the same projectRoot will both call LandBeadResult with stale baseRev assumptions; the current Merge implementation papers over conflicts with ort-strategy merge commits. The user was explicit in their feedback: "we may not be separating concerns appropriately — we have pushed too much functionality into execute-loop and it's causing us issues; it needs to be very simple: 'I've been handed a worktree and a task, I will execute that task and make commits, produce progress output, notify done.'" The current code does not reflect that intent.

4. Six open child beads of ddx-b7ee0d6f (bd42f640 fetch-before-claim, 6aa50e57 rebase-instead-of-merge, 83ec1430 push-on-land, 2791dc4f tracker concurrency, 42dd982c divergence monitoring, b0374901 multi-machine docs) each correctly identify one piece of the gap but do not compose into a coherent end-to-end workflow. The fix is not "implement all six"; the fix is to adopt the human-PR model as the single source of truth and then let those beads fall out as sub-tasks of a coherent design.

## Target design

The human-PR mental model, encoded as code:

1. **Worker.** Accepts a pre-made worktree and a prompt. Runs the agent harness. Emits structured progress events. Produces zero or more commits in the worktree. Exits with a simple terminal status (task_succeeded / task_failed / task_no_changes) and reports its worktree branch + commit SHAs to the server. The worker NEVER fetches, rebases, merges, pushes, or writes a ref. The worker NEVER interacts with target refs on the shared project repo. The worker does not care what main is doing.

2. **Land coordinator.** A single goroutine per project (hosted on the ddx server) that owns all writes to the project's target refs. Workers submit their completed worktree branch SHAs to the coordinator via a server endpoint. The coordinator, for each submission, in order: (a) `git fetch origin <target-branch>` to refresh the target tip; (b) `git rebase --onto <target-tip> <base-rev> <result-rev>` to replay the worker's commits onto the latest tip; (c) if the rebase is clean, `git push --ff-only origin <target-branch>` (or equivalent local update-ref if no remote); (d) on rebase conflict or push rejection, move the submission to `refs/ddx/iterations/<bead-id>/<attempt-id>` and mark the bead as needing human review. The coordinator handles one submission at a time. No locks needed internally because there's only one goroutine.

3. **Worker-to-coordinator handoff.** Workers submit to a new `POST /api/agent/workers/:id/land-submission` endpoint with {worktree_branch, base_rev, result_rev, bead_id}. The endpoint enqueues the submission onto the land coordinator's channel and returns 202 Accepted. Workers block (via long-polling GET, SSE, or channel-await) for the land result — success (pushed to target), preserved (moved to iteration ref), or failed (rejected, worker should retry from new tip).

4. **Worker isolation.** Workers run in /tmp/ddx-exec-wt/ worktrees per the alpha14 worktree-relocation fix. They fetch their starting base from the coordinator ("what is the current tip?"), branch off it, work, and submit. They never touch the shared project .git beyond their own worktree's read-fetch.

5. **Tracker (beads.jsonl) coordination.** Separate concern from the git target refs. ddx-2791dc4f stays as its own bead because the tracker is a second shared resource with its own write-serialization requirement. The land coordinator and the tracker lock must both be respected, but they are orthogonal locks.

6. **Multi-machine.** When the server grows to multi-machine topology, each machine runs its own land coordinator against its local clone of the project. Coordinators push to a common origin. The origin becomes the single source of truth for the target branch. Rebase-then-push gives the per-machine coordinators a clean serialization point via origin's ref update (push is atomic at the remote).

## What this replaces

- ddx-bd42f640 (fetch+ancestry check before claim) → subsumed. The land coordinator owns the fetch; workers don't need to check ancestry because they're handed a branch from the coordinator.
- ddx-6aa50e57 (rebase instead of merge) → subsumed. The coordinator's rebase step is the canonical implementation.
- ddx-83ec1430 (push-to-origin on landing) → subsumed. Push is part of the coordinator's sequence.
- ddx-42dd982c (divergence monitoring) → reframed. Becomes monitoring of the coordinator's rejection rate and conflict rate.
- ddx-b0374901 (multi-machine topology docs) → reframed. Document the per-machine coordinator + push-to-origin model.
- The missing "server runWorker never lands" bug (new P0 child, filed alongside) → subsumed. The new model HAS a land step by design.

What this does NOT replace:

- ddx-2791dc4f (beads.jsonl concurrent-write safety) — separate concern, separate lock.
- ddx-679d2fc7 (simplify worker to task-only) — this bead is nominally closed by commit 49c3d2b, but that commit did a cosmetic function split, not the architectural process split. Re-open or file a follow-up that cleans up the nomenclature ("OrchestratorGitOps" → "CoordinatorGitOps" scoped to the single coordinator goroutine) and deletes Merge() in favor of Land(). OR leave it closed and track that cleanup under this meta-bead.

## Why this is better

The core insight: **target-ref writes must be serialized through a single writer that always sees the current tip.** That is how every real PR queue works (GitHub merge queue, gerrit, Zuul). The current design has every worker trying to be its own merge-master; the fix is to have exactly one merge-master per project and let workers be contributors.

## Rollout

This is a redesign, not a patch. Rollout steps:

1. Land a new Land(projectRoot, baseRev, resultRev, targetBranch) coordinator function that implements fetch → rebase → ff-push → preserve-on-fail. Unit tests that cover the happy path, the rebase conflict path, and the concurrent-submission serialization path.
2. Add POST /api/agent/workers/:id/land-submission + the land coordinator goroutine + per-project queue channel to cli/internal/server/workers.go.
3. Rewrite workers.go:runWorker to have the worker's executor closure submit to the local coordinator (via channel; no HTTP round-trip needed since it's in the same process) instead of calling LandBeadResult inline.
4. Delete or mothball the current LandBeadResult and its "orchestrator" naming. The new coordinator replaces it.
5. Leave --local mode working by having it instantiate a process-local single-goroutine coordinator at startup. --local remains useful for single-bead debugging.
6. Add a --coordinator-only server mode for the multi-machine eventual topology (out of scope for the first cut; reference ddx-e2f497c3 for the design).

Out of scope for this bead (but related):

- Interactive conflict resolution UI. The meta-bead says "preserve under refs/ddx/iterations/" and move on; a separate UI flow for human merge-conflict resolution is its own work.
- Cross-machine coordinators talking to each other. Out of scope; origin push suffices.
- PR approval / review gates before the coordinator lands. Separate feature.
title: execute-loop: adopt human-PR landing model end-to-end (meta redesign)

acceptance: The design stack defines a single-project loop that watches execution-ready beads, consumes the generic DDx execution validator, executes beads in isolated worktrees, runs required post-run checks, preserves failed iterations with explicit bead/result state, lands successful runs by rebase+fast-forward, and is explicitly scoped for first release without depending on multi-project or multi-host server behavior
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: execute-bead automation and single-project worker supervision. Evidence covers FEAT-006, FEAT-010, SD-012, the execute-bead contract bead ddx-5448b39c, and the requirement to ship a tested single-project loop before broader multi-project or multi-host server work.
</context-digest>
Design the long-running single-project loop that watches for execution-ready beads and drives `ddx agent execute-bead` safely.

## Goals
1. Define a single-project loop that monitors one project queue for execution-ready beads using the generic DDx execution validator rather than HELIX-specific hidden policy
2. Define the exact state machine: claim/select bead, validate structural executability, execute in an isolated worktree, run post-run validity checks / required executions, classify outcome, and continue scanning
3. Make success and failure outcomes explicit: on failed execution or failed post-run validity checks, mark the bead/result accordingly and preserve the iteration/evidence exactly as the execute-bead contract specifies; on successful validation, land by rebase + fast-forward, reset the worker worktree to the updated branch tip, and continue
4. Keep the first shipped loop single-project scoped; do not require multi-project discovery, cross-project scheduling, or multi-host coordination in this bead
5. Define the minimal control surface for running the loop standalone or under `ddx server`, without coupling the design to later multi-project topology decisions

## Required outputs
- Single-project loop and worker state machine
- Failure/preservation semantics and retry surfaces
- Post-run validation / required-execution integration
- Minimal operator control surface and observability for the shipped loop
title: Design the single-project execute-bead loop and supervisor contract

acceptance: Invoking `ddx bead` commands anywhere inside a repo subtree that contains a local `.ddx/` resolves to that repo's tracker; installed and source-built binaries behave the same; and regression tests cover repo root and nested subdirectory invocation without silent success against the wrong tracker
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: tracker context resolution for ddx bead commands. Evidence covers the repo-local tracker contract in FEAT-004, the CLI project-root resolution rules, and the observed failure where an installed ddx binary invoked from /Users/erik/Projects/ddx returned bead IDs from `ddx bead create` but did not persist them into this repo's .ddx/beads.jsonl.
</context-digest>
Make bead commands resolve their tracker context deterministically from the nearest parent repository containing a .ddx directory, rather than drifting to another context or reporting success without updating the live tracker.

## Scope
- Reproduce and fix the context-resolution bug for `ddx bead create` and the rest of the bead command family
- Nail down the repository-selection rule: nearest parent repo with a local `.ddx/` wins
- Ensure installed and source-built binaries resolve the same tracker context when invoked from the same repo subtree
- Add regression coverage for repo-root, subdirectory, and nested-worktree invocation paths

## Boundaries
- Do not change the rule that bead state is maintained via `ddx bead` commands rather than direct JSONL edits
- Do not introduce hidden cross-project tracker selection heuristics

acceptance: ddx agent workers prints a table with STATE, BEAD ID, TITLE, HARNESS, MODEL, ELAPSED, ATTEMPTS columns. Server-submitted workers show state from WorkerManager. Local --local workers are detected via manifest scan of .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories. --json flag emits raw JSON. --watch re-renders every 2s. A worker executing bead axon-abc123 shows that bead ID and its title (truncated). An idle system (no active workers) prints 'no active workers' rather than an empty table. No dependency on worktrunk or any external binary beyond git.
description:
There is no CLI command to see which workers are running and which bead each is executing. The WorkerManager in cli/internal/server/workers.go already tracks this (WorkerRecord.CurrentBead at line 51, WorkerRecord.State, WorkerRecord.Harness, WorkerRecord.Model, WorkerRecord.Attempts/Successes/Failures) but it is only accessible through the server API. Local --local workers are not tracked by the server at all.

This bead adds ddx agent workers as a top-level CLI subcommand. It must not depend on worktrunk (wt) or any external tool.

## Required changes

### 1. New command: ddx agent workers (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go)
Register alongside the existing ddx agent log command. Flags:
  --json         emit raw JSON array
  --watch        re-render every 2s (like watch -n2) until Ctrl-C
  --project      filter to a specific project root (default: CWD project)

### 2. Data sources (two paths)
A. Server-submitted workers: GET http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/agent/workers — deserialize []WorkerRecord from the server. Already implemented in cli/internal/server/workers.go List() / HTTP handler.
B. Local --local workers: the server does not track these. Detect them by:
   1. Reading .ddx/workers/*/status.json files in the project root (server writes these even for local workers when they self-register — confirm in workers.go; if not, fall back to scanning active execute-bead worktrees)
   2. Scanning .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories (from ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go line 403) and reading the manifest.json inside each to extract bead_id, attempt_id, harness, model, started_at

### 3. Local worker detection via manifest scan
For each directory matching .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-*:
  - Read .ddx/executions/{attemptID}/manifest.json (path encoded in worktree name suffix)
  - Check if worktree is still active (git worktree list --porcelain)
  - Read execute-loop-heartbeat-at from bead.Extra (requires ddx-7651506a) if available
  - Derive synthetic WorkerRecord from manifest fields

### 4. Table output (default)
Columns: STATE | BEAD | TITLE (truncated 40 chars) | HARNESS | MODEL | ELAPSED | ATTEMPTS
- STATE: running (green), exited (dim), failed (red)
- ELAPSED: human-readable (e.g., 4m32s)
- If current bead title unavailable (local worker pre-manifest), show bead ID

### 5. Merge and deduplicate
When both server and manifest sources are available, deduplicate by worker_id or attempt_id. Server record takes precedence over manifest-derived record for the same worker.

## Key files
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — add newAgentWorkersCommand(), register in newAgentCommand()
- cli/internal/server/workers.go — WorkerRecord struct (lines 31-54), List API
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go — ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant (line 403), manifest shape (lines 726-771)
- cli/internal/agent/types.go — SessionEntry Correlation map (bead_id field)
title: Add ddx agent workers command — native live worker status with current bead

acceptance: FEAT-006, FEAT-002, and FEAT-008 define a structured embedded-agent progress contract for managed runs; the contract names the required phases and fields, distinguishes runtime-only progress from tracked execution evidence, and defines one shared read model for CLI and server/UI consumers
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: embedded ddx-agent runtime observability and live worker progress. Evidence covers FEAT-006 execution/evidence ownership, FEAT-002 server activity surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI expectations, execute-loop worker supervision, and the need for DDx-owned progress telemetry beyond coarse session logs when DDx runs the embedded harness.
</context-digest>
Define the progress-event contract for managed embedded-agent runs so DDx can surface meaningful live execution status in the CLI and server/UI.

## Goals
- Define structured phase/heartbeat output for embedded-agent runs (queueing, launch, running, post-checks, landing, preserved, done)
- Define the minimal per-event fields needed for operator visibility and later reporting: worker/project/bead/run identity, phase, elapsed/heartbeat timing, harness/provider/model/profile, and token/cost deltas when available
- Clarify which live progress artifacts remain runtime-only and which summaries/indexes are exposed through DDx-owned APIs and UI surfaces
- Keep provider-native transcript ownership external; DDx progress output should be normalized runtime telemetry, not transcript duplication

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-006, FEAT-002, and FEAT-008 so embedded-agent progress visibility is an explicit contract rather than an implementation detail
- Clarify how progress events relate to tracked execution artifacts, session logs, and server-managed worker state

## Required implementation planning
- Define the server/API/UI read model for live worker progress and recent phase history
- Define how CLI and UI consume the same structured progress feed without introducing hidden workflow policy

acceptance: The DDx pre-push `no-debug` hook runs without regex parse errors in a default ripgrep environment; the debug-scan command fails closed when the scan itself errors; and the hook still blocks actual debug statements it is meant to catch.
description:
<context-digest>
Observed during `git push` in /Users/erik/Projects/ddx after landing tracker commits for execute-loop alpha findings. The DDx pre-push hook `no-debug` invoked `rg` with a look-ahead pattern and emitted a regex parse error, but the hook summary still reported success and the push continued.
</context-digest>

The DDx pre-push `no-debug` hook uses an `rg` pattern that requires PCRE2 look-around support, but it invokes plain `rg` without `--pcre2`. In the current environment that produces a parse error while the hook still exits successfully.

## Reproduction
1. In `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx`, run `git push`.
2. Observe pre-push hook output from `no-debug` containing:
   `rg: regex parse error: ... look-around ... is not supported`
3. Observe the hook summary still marks `no-debug` as passed and the push continues.

## Why this matters
The hook currently gives a false sense of enforcement. A broken regex should either be written in ripgrep-compatible syntax or use `--pcre2`, and failures in the debug scan should not be silently treated as success.
title: Fix pre-push no-debug hook regex compatibility and failure semantics

acceptance: agent_runner mirror removed from DDx config schema; AGENT_* env vars apply in native config path; all tests pass
description:
Remove DDx's lossy embedded-agent config mirror and close the env-var forwarding gap in the native config path.

## Context

FEAT-006 defines native `.agent/config.yaml` as the authoritative config source for the embedded harness. The current `.ddx/config.yaml` `agent_runner` section is a deprecated mirror that duplicates native runtime config fields. Its precedence semantics are documented in FEAT-006 "Embedded Harness Configuration Ownership".

## Goals

- Remove `AgentRunnerConfig` type and `agent_runner` YAML fields from `.ddx/config.yaml` schema once no project uses it
- Remove `AgentYAMLConfig`, `LLMPresetYAML` mirror types from `cli/internal/agent/runner.go`
- Remove the `AgentConfigLoader` injection hook and `resolveAgentConfig` fallback path from `agent_runner.go`
- Apply `AGENT_*` env vars (`AGENT_MODEL`, `AGENT_PROVIDER`, `AGENT_BASE_URL`, `AGENT_API_KEY`, `AGENT_PRESET`) when resolving from native config, closing the gap documented in `TestConfigPrecedenceEnvVarAppliesInDdxFallbackPath`
- Wire the `agentRunner()` cmd factory to pass a working-directory path to native config resolution instead of loading the `agent_runner` block
- Update `TestConfigPrecedenceEnvVarAppliesInDdxFallbackPath` to verify env vars work in the native path after the fix

## Acceptance Criteria

- `go build ./...` and `go test ./...` pass with no regressions
- `.ddx/config.yaml` no longer accepts or uses `agent_runner` fields
- `AGENT_MODEL` set in the environment overrides the model resolved from native `.agent/config.yaml`
- Existing `.ddx/config.yaml` files with `agent_runner` blocks emit a deprecation warning or are silently ignored (no crash)
- The migration path in FEAT-006 remains valid

acceptance: New test TestRunWorker_ScriptHarness_LandsCommit in cli/internal/server/workers_test.go (or cli/internal/server/workers_land_test.go) that uses the script harness + integration helper to spawn one server-managed worker, waits for it to exit, and asserts: (1) record.LastResult.ResultRev matches `git log main -1 --format=%H` on the temp repo, (2) main has exactly one new commit from the initial seed, (3) the bead is closed with closing_commit_sha=record.LastResult.ResultRev, (4) no refs/ddx/iterations/ refs were created on the success path, (5) `git log --merges main | wc -l` is 0. Test must fail against current workers.go:runWorker (no LandBeadResult call) and pass after the fix. Fix is either (a) interim: add LandBeadResult + ApplyLandingToResult to the workers.go:248 closure using RealOrchestratorGitOps, or (b) canonical: route through the land coordinator from ddx-8746d8a6. Whichever is shipped, the test locks the contract: server-managed workers MUST advance the target branch. Runs under `go test ./internal/server -run RunWorker_ScriptHarness -race -count=1` in under 15s.
description:
The server path at cli/internal/server/workers.go:runWorker (around line 237) spawns worker goroutines whose inline executor closure (around line 248) calls agent.ExecuteBead directly and returns an ExecuteBeadReport — but never calls agent.LandBeadResult or any land coordinator. As a result, every commit produced by a server-managed execute-loop worker stays in its isolated worktree and never reaches the target branch. The loop reports success, closes the bead in the tracker, and the worktree is cleaned up — but `git log main` never sees the commit.

This is the parent bead ddx-e14efc58's bug. This child bead is specifically the REGRESSION TEST that locks the fix in place, and it depends on the script harness (ddx-025046ac) + integration helper (ddx-6f6bf12a) being in place.

## What this bead builds

A regression test in cli/internal/server/workers_test.go (or a new file cli/internal/server/workers_land_test.go) that:

1. Uses the integration helper from ddx-6f6bf12a to create a real temp git repo with a seeded bead
2. Instantiates a WorkerManager rooted at the temp repo
3. Submits ONE server-managed execute-loop worker with `--harness=script` and a directive file that appends a line and commits
4. Waits for runWorker to exit (via the existing signaling mechanism — close(progressCh), progressDone)
5. Asserts:
   - record.LastResult.ResultRev is non-empty and matches the script harness's commit SHA
   - `git log main -1 --format=%H` on the temp repo equals record.LastResult.ResultRev
   - `git rev-list main ^initial | wc -l` equals 1
   - ddx bead show returns status=closed with closing_commit_sha=record.LastResult.ResultRev
   - NO refs/ddx/iterations/* refs were created
   - `git log --merges main | wc -l` is 0 (linear history invariant)

The test MUST fail against today's workers.go:runWorker (which does not call LandBeadResult) and MUST pass after the fix. This catches the regression if anyone later re-introduces the gap.

## Fix scope

The fix is either:
- **Interim (smaller)**: mirror the --local closure at agent_cmd.go:1372 by calling LandBeadResult + ApplyLandingToResult inside the workers.go:248 closure, using RealOrchestratorGitOps. This uses the current merge-based path and produces 2-parent merge commits, but at least the work lands.
- **Canonical (larger)**: use the land coordinator from ddx-8746d8a6 once it exists — workers submit to the coordinator, coordinator rebases + ff-pushes, history stays linear. This is the meta-bead's design.

Prefer the canonical path if ddx-8746d8a6 has landed by the time this bead is worked. Otherwise ship the interim and note that ddx-8746d8a6 will replace it.

## Depends on

- ddx-025046ac (script harness) — the test uses it
- ddx-6f6bf12a (integration helper) — the test uses it
- ddx-8746d8a6 OR the interim LandBeadResult wiring — the production fix

## Out of scope

- Rewriting the entire workers.go runWorker (that's ddx-8746d8a6)
- Multi-project server tests (a separate concern)
- Client-side CLI changes (the CLI already handles local path correctly)
title: Server runWorker: land commits to target branch + script-harness regression test

description:
Sixth and final child of ddx-b7ee0d6f. The axon divergence happened partly because the multi-machine topology was never written down. Two machines (eitri and sindri) both ran execute-loop against the same project, neither was designated authoritative, and nobody had defined the pull/push contract. The result was predictable once you look at it, but there was no document that said 'this is wrong' before it happened.

## Required documentation

Write docs/EXECUTE-LOOP.md (or expand the ddx README) covering:

### Topology models

**Single-machine solo**: one machine, one execute-loop. No coordination needed beyond the fetch/push/rebase triad (ddx-bd42f640, ddx-83ec1430, ddx-6aa50e57).

**Primary + secondary(s)**: one machine is authoritative (pushes to origin). Secondaries pull-only — they can run the execute-loop if and only if the fetch bead guarantees they fast-forward from origin before every bead, and they refuse to land any work locally. Useful for running review/eval passes in parallel without forking.

**Peer mesh**: multiple machines all push to origin. Each must implement the fetch → verify ancestry → rebase → push invariant from the P0 beads. Requires the tracker concurrency bead (ddx-2791dc4f) to be solved or all machines will step on each other's beads.jsonl.

### Invariants

- Before claiming a bead: local HEAD must be a strict ancestor of origin/<default-branch> (or equal). If not, refuse to claim.
- After landing a bead successfully: push --ff-only or mark land_conflict and stop. Never leave work trapped locally.
- Between sibling workers on the same machine: rebase onto the latest master, never merge.
- Every bead claim must be visible to every other execute-loop before the claimer starts work (tracker concurrency).

### Operator runbook

- How to add a new worker machine safely (fresh clone + verify divergence is 0 + start loop)
- How to recover from a diverged state (the current axon situation): reconcile manually, reset secondaries to origin, restart loops
- How to migrate existing diverged projects to the new safety model (one-time cleanup)
- How to stop all execute-loops globally (e.g., during a freeze window)

### Troubleshooting

- 'divergence_detected' event: what caused it and how to recover
- 'push_rejected' event: what caused it and how to recover
- 'checkpoint before merge' commits in history: legacy pattern, should not appear after ddx-6aa50e57 lands
- Multiple 'Merge made by ort' commits: same as above, legacy pattern

### ADR reference

Once the tracker concurrency bead (ddx-2791dc4f) picks option A / B / C, cite the ADR here so operators understand the coordination model.

## Why this matters

The P0 beads will prevent future divergence, but operators need to understand WHY the rules exist to avoid bypassing them with --force or manual git operations. Documentation is what keeps the fixes effective a year from now.
title: execute-loop: document multi-machine topology and operator contract

acceptance: `ddx agent execute-loop` does not launch open epics or other structurally non-executable bead types by default; the generic execution-ready validation surface rejects or skips such beads before claim/launch; and deterministic tests cover a queue where an open epic sorts ahead of a ready task so the loop selects the task instead of the epic.
description:
<context-digest>
Alpha validation run from /Users/erik/Projects/helix using `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json` against a live HELIX queue. Evidence includes the generated execution bundle `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7`, worktree `.ddx/.execute-bead-wt-helix-fef22846-20260411T011535-bbda19d7`, and the current execute-loop implementation in `cli/internal/bead/store.go` and `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go`.
</context-digest>

`ddx agent execute-loop` selected and launched an open `epic` bead from the HELIX execution queue instead of a bounded executable bead.

## Reproduction
1. In `/Users/erik/Projects/helix`, run `ddx bead ready --json --execution`.
2. Observe the first candidate is `helix-fef22846`, `issue_type=epic`, title `Audit deleted HELIX artifact types for restoration or retirement`.
3. Run `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json`.
4. Observe DDx creates `.ddx/.execute-bead-wt-helix-fef22846-20260411T011535-bbda19d7` and `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/manifest.json` with `bead_id=helix-fef22846`.

## Observed gap
`ReadyExecution()` currently treats any open, unblocked bead as execution-ready unless `execution-eligible=false`, `superseded-by`, or cooldown metadata says otherwise. `execute-loop` then takes the first candidate without a stronger structural execution screen. That behavior is weaker than the API-001 execute-bead supervisor contract, which says the loop should run a generic execution-ready validator against ordered candidates before launch.

## Why this matters
Open epics represent umbrella planning/decomposition work, not necessarily a bounded workspace transform. Launching them directly makes queue-drain behavior unstable and can strand the loop on non-executable work even when bounded tasks are ready behind the epic.

DDx embeds or invokes ddx-agent and should move forward to the next released minor version after the agent release is published cleanly. This bead is intentionally queued now so the DDx-side adoption work is ready as soon as the upstream release is available.

## Upstream changes since the prior DDx adoption point
- acceptance-test coverage was expanded substantially across CLI, runtime, provider, routing, updater, installer, telemetry, and integration surfaces
- CI/build gates were hardened and unified: lint, gosec, govulncheck, format, normal tests, and race tests now share one canonical repo-local/CI command surface

- provider JSON/config output redaction was tightened for CLI security-scan compliance
- telemetry, retry ownership, session continuity, usage/reporting, and related runtime work landed earlier in the same release train and are now part of the released minor version

description:
Root cause epic for the multi-machine divergence problem discovered on axon 2026-04-14 during a planning session. Execute-loop treats master as a private branch and produces divergent histories that never reconcile with origin.

## Evidence

On axon, two machines running execute-loop (`eitri` and `sindri`) produced 14 days of independent history from a shared merge-base at 27c54ded (2026-04-06):

- eitri: 134 local commits that never reached origin
- sindri: 336 commits that pushed to origin
- Same FEAT-026 slot used for two unrelated features (sync protocol on eitri, markdown templates on sindri)
- Same beads.jsonl touched by both machines with divergent bead IDs and lifecycle state
- `refs/ddx/iterations/` preservation never fired because merges kept succeeding textually while producing semantically incoherent state
- 12+ "Merge made by 'ort' strategy" commits on 2026-04-13 afternoon on eitri alone, each bringing in a sibling local execute-bead worker's output

## Root causes identified

1. No `git fetch origin` at the top of a worker loop iteration — each bead starts from the worker's stale local HEAD (child bead DDX-agent-fetch)
2. No `git push` on successful landing — successful work stays trapped on the local branch (child bead DDX-agent-push)
3. Intra-machine sibling workers land via `git merge --no-edit` (ort strategy) instead of rebase, producing a fan of merge commits instead of a linear history (child bead DDX-agent-rebase)
4. .ddx/beads.jsonl is a git-tracked mutable ledger with no concurrent-write semantics (child bead DDX-agent-tracker)
5. No divergence monitoring — the 134/336 split grew silently over two weeks (child bead DDX-agent-monitor)
6. Multi-machine topology undocumented — which machine is authoritative, who pulls, who pushes (child bead DDX-agent-topology)

## Primary source evidence

- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go:59-69 — Merge function uses `git merge --no-edit` with 'ort' strategy, no rebase alternative, commits a 'chore: checkpoint before merge' before attempting each merge
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_commit.go — has ZERO references to push/fetch/origin
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:~320 — worker loop runs Executor.Execute without any fetch-origin or ancestry-check step

## Impact

Any project that has run the execute-loop for more than a few days across more than one machine is silently divergent from origin. Content written by this session cannot be pushed to the axon origin without a multi-hour manual reconciliation. The axon repo currently has two weeks of unshared work on two machines.

## Scope of this epic

Six child beads covering: fetch-before-work, push-on-success, rebase-vs-merge, tracker concurrency, divergence monitoring, multi-machine topology docs. Each is actionable and self-contained.

## Blocks

Any further `ddx agent execute-loop` runs on projects with more than one participating machine should be paused until at least the first three child beads (fetch, push, rebase) are landed. Running the loop in the current state compounds divergence.
notes:
## Reframed 2026-04-14 — design anchor: ddx-8746d8a6

The six original child beads (bd42f640, 6aa50e57, 83ec1430, 2791dc4f, 42dd982c, b0374901) identified pieces of the gap correctly but did not compose into a coherent end-to-end design. Per an opus-led architectural review on 2026-04-14, the root problem is not that execute-loop's git operations are missing pieces; it is that the whole model is wrong.

Target model (from the ddx user): human-PR queue. Workers are contributors, a centralized land coordinator is the maintainer, target-ref writes are serialized through a single writer that always sees the current tip.

The design for this model is in **ddx-8746d8a6 "execute-loop: adopt human-PR landing model end-to-end (meta redesign)"**. Treat ddx-8746d8a6 as the authoritative design anchor for this epic. The per-bug children below continue to describe individual gap points, but their FIX is the coordinator pattern, not piecemeal patches:

- ddx-bd42f640 (fetch+ancestry before claim) → subsumed by the coordinator's fetch step
- ddx-6aa50e57 (rebase instead of merge) → implemented by the coordinator's rebase step (add land-lock note to acceptance)
- ddx-83ec1430 (push on landing) → part of the coordinator's sequence
- ddx-42dd982c (divergence monitoring) → reframed as coordinator rejection/conflict metrics
- ddx-b0374901 (multi-machine docs) → reframed as per-machine coordinator + push-to-origin
- ddx-2791dc4f (tracker concurrency) → STAYS SEPARATE — tracker is a second shared resource with its own lock story

New P0 child filed alongside: **ddx-e14efc58 "server runWorker never calls LandBeadResult; commits silently lost"** — acute correctness bug discovered during the review. The server path today drops worker commits entirely. Interim fix mirrors the --local closure's land sequence; long-term fix is absorbed by ddx-8746d8a6.

Rollout: implement ddx-8746d8a6 first (the coordinator), then close the subsumed children as their work is done, then close this epic. Do NOT try to land the individual children as standalone patches — the piecemeal approach is what got us here.

Do NOT run execute-loop against the ddx repo (or any other repo) under this epic's pattern until the coordinator is in place. Record from the user: "we may not be separating concerns appropriately — worker should be 'I've been handed a worktree and a task, I will execute that task and make commits, produce progress output, notify done.'"
title: Epic: execute-loop git orchestration bugs cause multi-machine divergence

description:
First child of ddx-b7ee0d6f. Execute-loop workers currently start each bead from their local HEAD without verifying that HEAD is an ancestor of origin/master. When it isn't (because another machine pushed work), the worker produces commits that can never be fast-forward pushed, and the local branch silently diverges.

## Evidence

cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go around line 320 contains the worker-loop body:

    nextPhase("queueing", false)
    ...
    nextPhase("running", false)
    report, err := w.Executor.Execute(ctx, candidate.ID)

There is no `git fetch origin` and no ancestry check between these phases and the executor call. The worker enters Execute with whatever HEAD happens to be checked out.

Grep confirms: `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_commit.go` has zero references to push/fetch/origin. `execute_bead_orchestrator.go` has git merge/commit calls but never git fetch.

## Required behavior

Before claiming a bead (or at minimum before invoking the executor on a claimed bead), the worker must:

1. Run `git fetch origin <default-branch>` with a timeout. On network failure, log a structured warning and continue (so air-gapped runs still work), but record the staleness in the attempt metadata.
2. Run `git merge-base --is-ancestor HEAD origin/<default-branch>`. If HEAD is NOT an ancestor of origin, the local branch has diverged. This is a hard error:
   - Mark the bead as land_conflict and unclaim it
   - Emit a structured event 'divergence_detected' with both commits and the count
   - Stop the worker loop (do not pick another bead) until a human reconciles
3. If HEAD IS an ancestor but there are upstream commits (origin is ahead), fast-forward the local branch to origin before starting work. This rebases cleanly onto the latest server state.

## Test cases

- Fresh clone, no divergence: worker claims and runs normally
- Upstream is ahead by N commits, local has no uncommitted work: worker fast-forwards then runs
- Local is ahead of upstream (diverged): worker refuses to claim, emits divergence_detected, stops the loop
- `git fetch` times out / network unreachable: worker continues with a staleness warning
- Default branch is configurable (not always 'master') — config read from .ddx/config.yaml

## Related

Complementary to the push-on-success bead (sibling). Both are required for the loop to stay synchronized. Fetch alone is useless without push; push alone is useless without fetch.
title: execute-loop: fetch origin + verify ancestry before claiming a bead

description:
<ddx agent execute-loop injects z-ai/glm-5.1 model override that fails, while ddx agent run --harness agent works fine with qwen3.5-27b via vidar LMStudio host.>

ddx agent execute-loop on eitri consistently fails because the execute-loop → execute-bead path injects --model z-ai/glm-5.1, overriding both the ~/.config/agent/config.yaml default_provider (vidar → qwen3.5-27b) AND the models.yaml catalog. The same agent harness works perfectly with direct ddx agent run.

PROOF:

1. dd agent run --text "Hello" --harness agent --timeout 60s succeeds:
   - model: qwen3.5-27b
   - exit_code: 0
   - duration_ms: ~44s

2. ddx agent execute-loop --once --json --harness agent fails:
   - ps shows spawned process with --model z-ai/glm-5.1
   - result: execution_failed, 502 Bad Gateway

3. ddx agent execute-loop --once --json --harness agent --model qwen3.5-27b also fails:
   - Still gets a 404 from OpenRouter or 502 from Alibaba
   - execute-loop's --model flag doesn't propagate to the spawned execute-bead subprocess the way expected

ROOT CAUSE: execute-bead resolves the model from ddx metadata/routing (profiles, targets, or cached model catalog) rather than from ~/.config/agent/config.yaml or from execute-loop's --model flag. The z-ai/glm-5.1 model appears to come from a stale or incorrect routing data source.

EVIDENCE:
- models.yaml defines code-high profile with agent.openai: gpt-5.4
- ~/.config/agent/config.yaml defines vidar with qwen3.5-27b
- Neither source mentions z-ai/glm-5.1
- The model appears to come from a separate routing layer in execute-bead or execute-loop (possibly a cached catalog entry or the server's worker routing config)

FILES:
- cli/cmd/agent_execute_loop.go (execute-loop model resolution)
- cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go (execute-bead model resolution)  
- cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go (resolveModel chain)

acceptance: CLI help, operator docs, and ddx-bead skill guidance all teach the same model: execute-bead is the primitive, execute-loop is the normal queue-driven surface, planning/document-only beads are valid execution targets, merge is default unless explicit required execution docs block landing or --no-merge is set, and the documented JSON/result semantics tell operators and automation how close/preserve outcomes behave
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: consumer-facing execute-bead / execute-loop guidance. Evidence covers the shipped CLI help surfaces, the open docs gaps tracked by ddx-a4195a8e and ddx-7a01ba6c, and the need to teach humans and agents one coherent model: execute-bead is the primitive, execute-loop is the normal queue-driven surface.
</context-digest>
Implement the first complete consumer-facing guidance surface for DDx agent execution.

## Required changes
1. Update CLI help text and any nearby command docs so `ddx agent execute-bead` is described as the single-bead primitive and `ddx agent execute-loop` is described as the queue/supervisor command built on top of it.
2. Add or update user-facing operator docs and CLI reference surfaces so users discover `execute-loop` first for normal queue-driven work, with concrete examples for:
   - `ddx agent execute-loop`
   - `ddx agent execute-loop --once`
   - `ddx agent execute-loop --poll-interval <duration>`
   - `ddx agent execute-bead <bead-id>` for bounded/manual runs
3. Update the ddx-bead skill and any relevant internal skill/operator guidance so agents are told explicitly:
   - planning/document-only beads are valid execution targets
   - execute-loop is the default queue-driven entrypoint
   - execute-bead is the lower-level primitive for targeted/manual execution
4. Document the result/close semantics operators need in practice:
   - which outcomes/statuses close a bead
   - which outcomes preserve and leave the bead open
   - which JSON fields automation should read (`status`, `outcome`, `preserve_ref`, `result_rev`, `session_id`)
5. Make the operator guidance consistent with the current merge model:
   - merge is the default successful outcome
   - explicit required execution documents are the automatic merge gates
   - `--no-merge` is the manual preserve override

## Boundaries
- Do not invent a new bead shape or merge-gate field
- Use the existing execute-bead / execute-loop / execution-document contract
- Keep the user-facing guidance aligned with the actual shipped command behavior and documented result surface
title: Implement execute-loop-first help, skill, and operator guidance

acceptance: The bead tracker has fuzz/property/chaos coverage that hammers CRUD, deps, claims, closes, evidence appends, malformed JSONL, and duplicate-row repair paths; randomized runs preserve core invariants (parseable file, one live row per bead ID, valid statuses/deps, deterministic ready/blocked behavior); and the new coverage is easy to rerun locally when tracker bugs are suspected
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: bead tracker durability and corruption resistance. Evidence covers the existing concurrency/chaos tests in cli/internal/bead/chaos_test.go, the recent duplicate-row/latest-state bugs, and the need to aggressively validate JSONL tracker safety under randomized updates, crashes, and malformed/interleaved state.
</context-digest>
Add deeper destructive test coverage for the bead tracker so we can trust it under queue-worker churn.

## Scope
- Add property/state-machine style tests for bead CRUD, deps, claim/unclaim, close/reopen, and evidence append invariants
- Add fuzz coverage for JSONL parsing, duplicate-row folding, and tracker round-trip safety
- Expand chaos tests to mix concurrent update/claim/close/create/evidence operations against the same store
- Verify the tracker never returns corrupt JSONL, duplicate live IDs, or impossible state transitions after randomized operation streams
- Add any minimal repair/canonicalization assertions needed to prove the live view remains one current state per bead ID

## Boundaries
- Focus on the tracker/store and queue derivation surfaces, not broader agent execution behavior
- Prefer deterministic seeds and reproducible shrinkable failures where possible
title: Harden bead tracker with fuzz, property, and chaos coverage

acceptance: After a `no_changes` execute-loop result where `base_rev == result_rev`, the bead persists `execute-loop-retry-after`, `execute-loop-last-status`, and `execute-loop-last-detail`; `ddx bead ready --json --execution` suppresses the bead until the retry-after time; and a deterministic test covers the tracker-backed path rather than only in-memory worker logic.
description:
<context-digest>
Follow-up observation from the HELIX alpha run that launched `helix-fef22846` via `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json` and returned `status=no_changes`, `detail=agent made no commits`, `base_rev=result_rev=de67981a79a27ab8449bf6c475dda677fb25acfc`.
</context-digest>

`ddx agent execute-loop` reported a no-progress result but did not persist the retry-suppression metadata that `ReadyExecution()` expects, so the same bead remained at the front of the execution-ready queue immediately after the attempt.

## Reproduction
1. In `/Users/erik/Projects/helix`, run `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json` and get a `no_changes` result where `base_rev == result_rev`.
2. Inspect bead `helix-fef22846` in `.ddx/beads.jsonl` or via `ddx bead show --json`.
3. Observe the new `execute-bead` event is present, but `execute-loop-retry-after`, `execute-loop-last-status`, and `execute-loop-last-detail` are absent.
4. Run `ddx bead ready --json --execution` again.
5. Observe `helix-fef22846` is still the first execution-ready candidate instead of being suppressed for the cooldown window.

## Observed gap
`ExecuteBeadWorker.Run()` calls `SetExecutionCooldown()` for no-progress outcomes, but the tracker-backed HELIX run did not retain any of the cooldown fields that `ReadyExecution()` checks.

## Why this matters
Without durable cooldown metadata, `execute-loop` can repeatedly burn agent time and tokens on the same no-op bead in back-to-back scans.
title: Persist execute-loop cooldown metadata after no-progress attempts

acceptance: DDx updates its ddx-agent dependency/reference to upstream v0.1.1; any required integration or config changes for retry ownership, telemetry, usage/session continuity, or tool path behavior are implemented; affected tests pass; and any noteworthy compatibility or rollout notes are recorded in the bead or linked docs
description:
Upstream `DocumentDrivenDX/agent` now has point release `v0.1.1`. Update DDx to consume that release and reconcile any integration assumptions that changed since `v0.1.0`.

Key upstream changes since `v0.1.0`:
- OTel telemetry landed for session, chat-attempt, and tool spans/metrics, with a documented telemetry contract and runtime-specific cost attribution.
- Provider retries moved into the runtime so providers execute one attempt per call and expose richer attempt metadata.
- Session continuity/history support landed in `Run`.
- `ddx-agent usage` reporting landed.
- Tool path handling now resolves symlink chains.
- Compaction behavior was hardened: prefix-aware accounting, balanced compaction events, and fail-closed handling when compaction cannot fit within budget.
- Self-update cache writing was fixed for first-run homes without an existing cache directory.
- Mixed-provenance known-cost session rollups now preserve the root known cost instead of downgrading to unknown.

DDx should review whether any wrapper logic, telemetry ingestion, usage/reporting integration, or runtime assumptions need adjustment when moving from `v0.1.0` to `v0.1.1`.
notes: Bumped github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent to v0.1.1 in cli/go.mod; no DDx wrapper/source changes were required because the v0.1.1 API remained compatible. Verified with 'go test ./internal/agent ./cmd -count=1' in the cli module.
title: Update DDx to ddx-agent v0.1.1

acceptance: execute-bead synthesizes a deterministic prompt file from bead data when `--prompt` is omitted; the synthesized prompt includes title, description, acceptance, and available governing metadata instead of only the bead id; execute-loop inherits that contextual prompt automatically; `--prompt` still overrides synthesis; and automated tests fail if the default prompt regresses to the bare `Work on bead <id>.` fallback or if the runner stops receiving the synthesized context.
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: execute-bead default prompt construction. Evidence covers cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go falling back to the literal prompt `Work on bead <id>.`, FEAT-006 requiring execute-bead to resolve the bead and governing artifacts before launch, and a real no-change Codex run in /Users/erik/Projects/agent where the harness received only the bead id and wandered the repo.
</context-digest>
Fix execute-bead so omitted `--prompt` no longer produces an under-specified agent task.

## Required implementation
1. Replace the bare string fallback in `cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go` with deterministic prompt synthesis driven by the bead record.
2. Build the default prompt from durable bead context:
   - bead id and title
   - description
   - acceptance criteria
   - relevant metadata such as `spec-id`, parent, labels, and any declared governing references when available
3. Materialize that synthesized prompt as a prompt file in the managed execution worktree instead of relying on shell-fragile inline quoting.
4. Keep `--prompt` as an explicit override; only synthesize the default prompt when the caller did not supply one.
5. Make `execute-loop` inherit the same behavior automatically by continuing to call `execute-bead` without needing its own prompt-building fork.
6. Add regression coverage proving the default path no longer sends only `Work on bead <id>.` and that the runner receives the synthesized bead context.

## Boundaries
- Do not invent a new bead schema.
- Do not move prompt assembly into HELIX-specific logic.
- Use DDx-owned bead/store/docgraph context only.
notes: Implemented. buildPrompt() synthesizes from bead title/description/acceptance/governing refs when --prompt is omitted. Regression test at cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go:520 asserts the fallback 'Work on bead <id>.' prompt is never used.
title: Give execute-bead a deterministic bead-context prompt

description:
<context-digest>
Review area: ddx server topology. Evidence covers FEAT-002, FEAT-013, architecture.md, TP-002, and the current assumption that one `ddx server` serves exactly one repo.
</context-digest>
Design the first-step multi-project `ddx server` module for a single machine.

## Goals
1. Replace the single-project server assumption with a project registry / project-root collection managed by one server process on one machine
2. Define project-scoped API, MCP, and UI routing semantics so clients can enumerate projects and then operate within one selected project context
3. Define per-project config, cache, and adapter boundaries so one bad project cannot poison other served projects
4. Define how project-scoped worktrees and worker pools are managed under one server process without assuming only one active repository
5. Define migration from today's single-project `ddx server` invocation and data adapters without breaking current local use

## Required outputs
- Project registry/discovery contract
- Request routing and project selection model
- Per-project isolation and failure-mode analysis
- Impact on FEAT-008 UI and TP-002 end-to-end coverage
title: Design one-machine multi-project ddx server topology

Loads /nodes/:nodeId/agents. Asserts: (1) all three workers appear in the table, (2) each row has a non-empty 'Project' column matching the seeded project name, (3) row order is by started-at descending, (4) clicking a row navigates to /nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/agents/:workerId with the URL params matching the clicked worker's project and id, (5) when a worker's state transitions from running to exited the table updates within one refresh cycle. description:
When an operator has N projects in flight (e.g. ddx, axon, helix) the FEAT-021 routing puts each project's workers under /nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/agents. To see what's running across ALL projects you currently have to click into each project separately. Add a node-level (combined) view that lists every running worker on the node regardless of project, so the operator has one situational-awareness pane.

Backing data: GET /api/agent/workers already returns ALL workers managed by the server (it's a node-wide endpoint, not scoped to a single project). The UI just needs to render this list with a project column.

UI requirements:
- Route: /nodes/:nodeId/agents (combined; sibling of /nodes/:nodeId/beads and /nodes/:nodeId/sessions)
- Columns: project name, worker id, bead id, harness, model, state, started-at, attempts, last-status
- Auto-refresh every ~3s
- Each row links to /nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/agents/:workerId for the per-worker detail (which is ddx-7be82986)
- Project picker dropdown should also expose 'All projects' as a value that navigates to this page
- Empty-state message when zero workers are running

acceptance: FEAT-006, FEAT-002, and FEAT-008 define a structured embedded-agent progress contract for managed runs; the contract names the required phases and fields, distinguishes runtime-only progress from tracked execution evidence, and defines one shared read model for CLI and server/UI consumers
description:
<context-digest>
Review area: embedded ddx-agent runtime observability and live worker progress. Evidence covers FEAT-006 execution/evidence ownership, FEAT-002 server activity surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI expectations, execute-loop worker supervision, and the need for DDx-owned progress telemetry beyond coarse session logs when DDx runs the embedded harness.
</context-digest>
Define the progress-event contract for managed embedded-agent runs so DDx can surface meaningful live execution status in the CLI and server/UI.

## Goals
- Define structured phase/heartbeat output for embedded-agent runs (queueing, launch, running, post-checks, landing, preserved, done)
- Define the minimal per-event fields needed for operator visibility and later reporting: worker/project/bead/run identity, phase, elapsed/heartbeat timing, harness/provider/model/profile, and token/cost deltas when available
- Clarify which live progress artifacts remain runtime-only and which summaries/indexes are exposed through DDx-owned APIs and UI surfaces
- Keep provider-native transcript ownership external; DDx progress output should be normalized runtime telemetry, not transcript duplication

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-006, FEAT-002, and FEAT-008 so embedded-agent progress visibility is an explicit contract rather than an implementation detail
- Clarify how progress events relate to tracked execution artifacts, session logs, and server-managed worker state

## Required implementation planning
- Define the server/API/UI read model for live worker progress and recent phase history
- Define how CLI and UI consume the same structured progress feed without introducing hidden workflow policy

description:
<context-digest>
Review area: host+user ddx-server planning stack after the server model evolved beyond the earlier multi-project topology draft. Evidence covers FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, TP-002, the fresh-eyes server review, and the updated requirements for a per-user host daemon, localhost HTTP UI, SQLite-backed runtime/index state, embedded-agent progress visibility, provider dashboarding, replay-backed fixtures, and Playwright coverage for every new UI surface.
</context-digest>
Update the governing server planning docs so they describe the current intended host+user ddx-server model before follow-on server design and implementation beads continue.

## Goals
- Reconcile FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, and TP-002 around a single host+user ddx-server instance with explicit project registry, worker management boundaries, and queue semantics
- Define the split between git-backed project truth, tracked execution evidence, and host-local runtime/index state backed by an embedded database
- Define the current transport/security model: localhost unauthenticated HTTP for UI/API now, explicit future transport/auth evolution later
- Add the embedded-agent progress, provider dashboard, replay-backed fixture, and Playwright coverage requirements to the governed server plan
- Make the scheduling boundary explicit: server may allocate workers across projects, but bead ordering remains owned by each project queue

## Boundaries
- Keep Axon and multi-host service-backed topology as later-stage work
- Do not reopen the already-shipped single-project execute-bead loop contract except where the new server plan must reference it

acceptance: A regression test covers a mixed log directory containing both routing-outcomes.jsonl and legacy sessions.jsonl rows, and ddx agent usage includes both data sources instead of dropping legacy sessions when routing outcomes are present.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cli/cmd/agent_usage.go:44
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 83700bf switches ddx agent usage to prefer routing-outcomes.jsonl, but the command only falls back to sessions.jsonl when there are zero routing-derived rows at all. In a mixed log directory, as soon as one new routing outcome exists, all older legacy session rows are ignored. That breaks the recorded requirement to preserve backward compatibility for existing activity/session rows and will under-report historical usage until every old session has been replayed into the new store.
Suggested fix: Merge routing-metrics rows with legacy sessions.jsonl rows during the compatibility window, or keep using legacy rows for historical coverage until a complete migration marker exists; add regression coverage for mixed old/new log directories.
title: integration: preserve legacy session history in agent usage
